In complex matrices rich in organic foulants, use short deposition times (30–60 s) with SWV to minimize electrode fouling31,46,47. The overall analytical response of a voltammetric sensor is critically influenced by a combination of operational conditions and material-related characteristics of the sensing interface. Effective tuning of these parameters is fundamental to maximizing signal-to-noise ratio, controlling matrix-related interferences, and attaining the lowest practicable detection limits for the heavy metal analytes. Each of the key parameters governing voltammetric performance can be described in detail, and their respective roles can be outlined for applications in trace heavy metal analysis. The deposition potential corresponds to the fixed potential used during the preconcentration stage; it must be adequately negative in ASV to drive efficient electroreduction and immobilization of the metal ions on the electrode surface48. For example, nano-cellulose modified electrodes were used for the detection of Cd(II), Cu(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II), where an optimum deposition potential of −0.90 V was selected to ensure efficient preconcentration of all metal ions48. The deposition time, which specifies how long preconcentration is maintained, is directly proportional to the analyte loading on the electrode and therefore improves sensitivity with reduced detection limits; although excessive deposition periods lengthen the analysis and can induce electrode fouling49. The scan rate represents the rate of potential change in the stripping phase and influences both peak height and resolution, as faster scans usually yield larger peak currents yet tend to broaden peaks and diminish the separation between closely spaced features50. For example, in this study a mercury working electrode was employed for the simultaneous determination of Tl(I), Pb(II) and Cu(II), and it was shown that increasing the scan rate in DPV enhanced the peak current but led to peak broadening and poorer separation of the metal signals; thus an intermediate scan rate of 10 mV/s was chosen to balance sensitivity and resolution50. The pulse amplitude, which denotes the height of the potential steps in DPV or SWV, boosts faradaic current and analytical sensitivity as it is raised, but at the cost of a higher background signal and potential peak shape distortion51. Finally, the electrode material, including both its surface morphology and chemical composition, plays a central role in governing selectivity, controlling overpotential, and facilitating efficient preconcentration through adsorption or complexation, thereby strongly affecting the sensor's sensitivity and robustness52,53. Traditional electrode materials have historically underpinned electrochemical strategies for the detection and removal of heavy metals in water treatment applications. Within analytical chemistry, their use has been especially prominent in stripping voltammetry, where their characteristic physicochemical behavior facilitates highly sensitive and selective measurement of toxic metal ions. Although recent research increasingly prioritizes alternative electrode materials in response to toxicity and cost issues, a clear understanding of the performance and constraints of these conventional systems remains essential for interpreting contemporary advances. Mercury-based electrodes have been widely valued for their elevated hydrogen overpotential and their renewable, smooth surface, attributes that render them especially well-suited to ASV54. Mercury-based electrodes, including the hanging mercury drop electrode (HMDE) and the dropping mercury electrode (DME), have long been recognized for their outstanding analytical performance. Their capacity to form amalgams with a wide range of metal ions enables preconcentration of the analyte before the stripping stage, thereby achieving very low detection limits. Historically, the bubbling mercury electrode illustrated this advantage by enabling the determination of zinc, cadmium, lead, and copper in natural and wastewater samples, where a clear proportionality between analyte concentration and peak current was established54. Additionally, mercury electrodes face several limitations, including contamination in complex sample matrices, time-consuming cleaning procedures, and poor long-term stability. Consequently, research into sustainable alternatives has accelerated. Mercury-free materials aim to match or exceed the analytical performance of mercury-based electrodes while reducing both environmental and health hazards55. Despite the advantageous physicochemical features of mercury and its broad use in trace-level heavy metal analysis, both the dropping mercury and HMDEs exhibit insufficient mechanical stability for automated applications, since their droplets can detach easily. The substantial consumption of elemental mercury in these systems also contradicts current efforts toward sustainable analytical chemistry, where carbon-based and surface-modified materials are favored to improve analytical sensitivity56,57. A study focusing on a rice agroecosystem employed HMDE for the simultaneous determination of trace metals and reported highly favorable analytical figures of merit. The simultaneous determination of Zn(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II) was performed with a HMDE in 0.1 M sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.5). For each metal, the anodic stripping peak heights varied linearly over the concentration range 20-100 µg/L. The limits of detection were 4 µg/L for Cd, 10 µg/L for Pb, and 10 µg/L for Zn. Collectively, these data demonstrate the electrode's robustness for multi-element analysis at trace levels pertinent to water quality assessment58. One reported approach utilized a thin-film mercury electrode (TFME) formed on carbon tips to simultaneously measure Cd(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II). This electrode architecture not only enabled multi-metal analysis but also improved sensitivity and achieved lower limits of detection compared to unmodified electrodes, while the experimental results were accurately captured by an artificial neural network model \(\left({R}^{2},|,0.9933\right)\)59. One critical investigation of mercury-film-modified commercial screen-printed electrodes (SPEs) for Pb(II) sensing reported a satisfactory LOD of 8.9 µg/L in ultrapure spiked water using ASV, but also highlighted problems associated with non-uniform mercury deposition on the rough electrode substrate60. Furthermore, the use of TFMEs, produced by electrodeposition on solid supports such as glassy carbon, represents one method for trace metal analysis. A study utilized low-current stripping chronopotentiometry (SCP) to quantify Fe(III) in filtered coastal and estuarine waters61. Despite its high sensitivity, the approach required multiple pre-treatment procedures, notably filtration and complexation with solochrom violet. The same electrochemical setup was also effective for detecting Sb(III) at ultra-trace levels in ultrapure simulated water, achieving a detection limit of 0.008 µg/L (3σ method), and was successfully applied to seawater samples using the standard addition method. Furthermore, the implementation of a double electrolysis potential during the preconcentration step effectively reduced interference from copper62. According to a study63, the mercury film electrode exhibited inadequate sensitivity and reproducibility when applied to Se(IV) analysis. The inclusion of Cu(II) ions in the plating process on the GC electrode effectively resolved this limitation. The dissolved metallic copper in the Hg film was identified as the key factor responsible for current peak intensification. The optimized method yielded a LOD of 0.06 µg/L in deionized water/0.1 M HNO3 (signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 2) and a relative standard deviation of 5.2% (n = 5) for 1 µM Se(IV) determined by CSV63. Another study developed a potentiometric stripping analysis technique enabling the concurrent quantification of Cu(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II) in seawater samples64. In this method, a mercury film was electrodeposited directly onto a rotating glassy carbon electrode before each measurement by applying a potential of −1.1 V vs SCE for 10 min. Following the deposition, an electrolysis-stripping sequence was executed. The resulting metal concentrations were benchmarked against a mixed standard solution containing the same analytes. The method achieved detection limits (2σ method) of 0.044 µg/L for Cu(II), 0.003 µg/L for Pb(II), and 0.001 µg/L for Cd(II), all evaluated from a natural seawater sample, although the total analysis time was relatively long at about 75 min. Despite their exceptional and renewable atomically smooth surface that provides strong selectivity, the performance profile of mercury electrodes collapses under the criteria of operational durability and translational feasibility. Their mechanical instability prevents automation, while time-consuming cleaning and matrix contamination further limit practical use; moreover, long-term stability and reproducibility are compromised by non-uniform film depositions on solid substrates55,56,62,65,66. Critically, their intrinsic toxicity necessitates stringent handling, storage, and disposal protocols, increasing operational cost and precluding field-deployable, single-use sensing formats. Collectively, these limitations have driven a decisive shift away from this class, driving a transition to solid-state electrodes. Carbon electrodes represent a fundamental class of materials in electrochemical sensor technology for heavy metal detection, providing an advantageous combination of electrochemical efficiency, adaptability, and environmental sustainability. These electrodes are the most versatile and widely adopted solid-state platforms for heavy metal voltammetry, yet their analytical performance cannot be generalized; it is fundamentally dictated by the choice of carbon substrate, the strategy used to modify its interface, and the electrochemical protocol employed. Their remarkable characteristics-high electrical conductivity, substantial specific surface area, and modifiable surface chemistry-make them particularly effective for the sensitive and selective quantification of toxic metal ions such as Pb(II), Cd(II), and Hg(II) in environmental samples67. Typically, the detection process entails an initial enrichment of metal ions on the electrode surface driven by adsorption or electrostatic forces, succeeded by ASV measurement that generates distinct current signals correlated with analyte concentration68. This strategy overcomes key drawbacks of traditional spectroscopic methods, which, although highly accurate, depend on costly instruments, laborious sample pretreatment, and are generally unsuitable for in situ applications69. The raw analytical performance is attributable to the modifier, and advances in nanomaterial science have markedly improved the analytical capabilities of carbon-based electrodes, with graphene and its derivatives, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and emerging nanocomposites being widely investigated as electrode modifiers70. For example, a biosensor based on GO composite onto screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) was reported for heavy metal detection. The LOD (Inhibitory Concentration (IC15)) for Hg2⁺, Pb2⁺, and Cd2⁺ were 0.0075 µg L−1, 0.015 µg L−1, and 0.0312 µg L−1, respectively, determined in buffered measurement solution71. Similarly, functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have enabled Pb(II) detection with a LOD of 2.1 µg/L in simulated double distilled water68. Furthermore, advanced sensor platforms that integrate artificial intelligence with carbon nanomaterial-based electrodes have demonstrated outstanding performance, enabling the concurrent determination of several metal ions with limits of detection of 1.6 µg/L for Pb(II) and 1.9 µg/L for Hg(II) using the 3σ method69. SPCEs and carbon paste electrodes (CPEs) have attracted considerable interest due to their suitability for portable, field-deployable sensing platforms. Somerset and co-workers fabricated conducting polymer-coated SPCEs and modified CPEs for the electrochemical determination of Hg(II), Pb(II), Ni(II), and Cd(II). The SPCE modified with a polyaniline-poly(2,2'-dithiodianiline) (PANI-PDTDA) film exhibited an exceptionally low LOD of 4.41 × 10−5 µg/L in laboratory model solutions (calculated using the 3σ method), with linearity observed across the concentration range of 0.20–200 µg/L72. Sensitivity and LOD are thus governed not by the substrate itself, but by interfacial design and the reproducibility of the modification step. Selectivity in carbon-based sensors is not intrinsic to the substrate but is conferred by the modifier's chemical functionality, typically through coordination-based analyte chelation or electrostatic exclusion of matrix interferents. For instance, a sulfur-doped graphitic carbon nitride with sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (QDs) nanocomposite modified SPCE demonstrated excellent sensitivity for Hg(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II). The sensor exhibited wide linear ranges of approximately 1.50–300 µg/L for Hg(II), 16–600 µg/L for Cu(II), and 26–1100 µg/L for Pb(II), with corresponding limits of detection of 0.56 µg/L, 6.0 µg/L, and 10.0 µg/L (S/N = 3), respectively, measured in spiked ultrapure water73. This performance is fundamentally governed by the modifier's chemistry: the sulfur-doped nanocomposite provides high-affinity coordination sites that promote preconcentration and selective stripping analysis, while the carbon QD expands the active surface area and accelerates charge transfer. Practical applicability was demonstrated through good recoveries (95.3–106.4%) in tap water and juice samples. It should be noted that the robustness of this selectivity in unspiked environmental matrices containing humic substances, colloidal particles, and high levels of competing ions was not investigated in the study, leaving the sensor's performance under such field-relevant conditions to be established in future work. From another perspective, the use of a porous carbon-fiber microelectrode coated with gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) enabled the parallel quantification of Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II). The LOD were determined in spiked deionized water to be 127 µg/L, 294 µg/L, 61.4 µg/L, and 148 µg/L for Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II), respectively, each within its own linear concentration range (1124–12364 µg/L for Cd(II), 2072–22792 µg/L for Pb(II), 636–6991 µg/L for Cu(II), and 2006–22066 µg/L for Hg(II)). No interference between the metals was observed, and the method was further validated for simultaneous determination in lake water samples using the standard addition approach74. This study shows that modest \({\rm{LOD}}\) values can be offset by real-sample validation using standard addition, which substantially strengthens confidence in sensor performance and should be benchmarked across material classes. GCEs are extensively employed as a basic working electrode for electrochemical analysis of heavy metal ions in wastewater, attributed to their excellent conductivity, wide operational potential range, minimal background current, and robust chemical inertness75. In trace-level determinations, the electrode's performance is generally optimized by surface modification with carefully selected materials to boost sensitivity and selectivity, frequently employing nanomaterials like graphene, metallic nanoparticles, and polymeric phases, or constructing composite films that expand the effective surface area and furnish specific binding domains for the analytes of interest76. The versatility of the GCE enables a wide range of functionalization approaches, establishing it as a fundamental platform in designing sensors for the determination of pollutants such as lead, cadmium, mercury, and arsenic in complex environmental matrices77,78. They offer outstanding analytical performance across a broad spectrum of metal ions and can be readily modified; notably, very low LOD values are attainable when a GCE is employed. For instance, one study employed a simple Bi-film coating on the electrode surface, enabling detection limits in double-distilled synthetic water (S/N ratio of 3 method) of approximately 1.07, 0.93, 0.65, and 0.94 µg/L for Zn, Cd, Pb, and Cu, respectively. The analysis of real river samples showed good agreement with AAS results, without compromising accuracy or repeatability79. In one reported Pb(II) determination, a GCE functionalized with a graphene/Nafion composite layer afforded a linear dynamic range from 4.1 to 5000 µg/L, with a detection limit of 1.1 µg/L (3σ method) determined in spiked deionized water using DPV80. The Nafion composites on a GCE use the cation-exchange properties of Nafion to impart anti-interference capabilities and sensitivity in spiked river water. Even higher sensitivity was obtained when GO was covalently immobilized on the GCE, providing linear calibration ranges for Pb(II) and Cd(II) from 2.1 µg/L down to 2.1 × 10⁻⁴ µg/L and from 1.1 µg/L down to 1.1 × 10⁻⁴ µg/L, respectively. The corresponding limits of detection were remarkably low, at 5.18 × 10⁻⁵ µg/L for Pb(II) and 3.15 × 10⁻⁵ µg/L for Cd(II), as determined in spiked ultrapure water81. Among the latest advances is a pyrolytic glassy carbon-graphene composite electrode integrated within a microfluidic system, which attains detection limits of 0.020 µg/L for Pb(II) and 0.100 µg/L for Cd(II) in simulated deionized water (S/N ratio method). This configuration highlights how sensor architecture can be finely optimized to achieve sub-parts-per-trillion sensitivity78. In the cited studies, stability is typically assessed over only a few consecutive cycles or days, with no quantitative data on signal decay over weeks of continuous immersion or repeated use in contaminated matrices. Similarly, the resistance to common fouling agents, such as humic and fulvic acids, is seldom quantified; where real-water validation exists, it often relies on standard addition methods that do not isolate fouling effects from other matrix interferences. This lack of data represents a significant research gap, as it prevents a meaningful comparison with the inherent anti-fouling advantage offered by disposable SPCEs. Furthermore, the scalability of GCE modification processes, often involving manual drop-casting or in-situ electrodeposition, raises concerns about batch-to-batch reproducibility at a manufacturing level, an aspect that must be standardized before GCE-based sensors can transition from laboratory prototypes to commercial products. Here, the carbon sub-classes sharply diverge, defining a clear trade-off in field deployability. SPCEs are inherently mass-producible using thick-film and printing technologies, offering low-cost, disposable platforms ideal for on-site sensing, which eliminates cumulative electrode fouling and cross-contamination concerns. Conversely, GCEs, while offering superior baseline performance due to their robust chemical inertness, are typically non-disposable polishing-dependent electrodes. Their performance for trace metal detection is thus markedly dependent on antifouling resistance and long-term stability of the modifying film, a critical property that is rarely subjected to rigorous, quantitative evaluation in the cited literature, representing a significant research gap. The cost-effectiveness of GCE-based sensors is high per-electrode but low per-measurement if reusability is proven; SPCEs offer the opposite trade-off. In electrochemical analysis of heavy metal ions in water, gold electrodes now represent a key enabling technology, distinguished by their superior conductivity, favorable biocompatibility, and reproducible electrochemical characteristics. These electrodes can be employed in forms varying from simple bare gold to elaborately nanostructured systems, with each design tailored to enhance trace-level detection. Due to the intrinsic interaction of gold with specific metals, particularly mercury and arsenic, highly sensitive direct measurements are possible, and the chemical stability of gold as a support material allows the construction of advanced, functionally diverse sensing interfaces82,83. Modifying gold electrodes with nanostructured materials, such as AuNPs or nanoclusters, leads to a substantial improvement in analytical capability. The increased surface-to-volume ratio and elevated electrocatalytic activity of these nanomaterials generate a higher abundance of active sites that facilitate metal ion accumulation and electron transfer83,84. For example, a gold electrode functionalized with gold nanoclusters (GNPs-Au) has shown remarkable sensitivity for the concurrent determination of lead and cadmium. The sensor provides a broad linear calibration range of 1–250 µg/L and an ultra-low detection limit of 0.001 µg/L for both analytes, measured in spiked ultrapure water, underscoring the effectiveness of nanoscale surface modification. The results show only minor deviations from those obtained by AAS, indicating high accuracy and reliability84. Similarly, nanocomposite strategies employing electrodes modified with graphene QD and gold nanoparticles have demonstrated excellent performance toward other toxic metals, such as mercury, achieving detection limits (S/N ratio method) down to 0.004 µg/L83. In addition, researchers facile sensing platform by electrochemically co-depositing a gold nanofilm onto a screen-printed carbon electrode for the detection of trace mercury (II). The sensor, tuned through response surface methodology, demonstrated a linear response from 1 to 40 µg/L and, after optimization of chloride conditions, achieved a highly sensitive detection limit of 0.16 µg/L (3σ method) in simulated deionized water. It also showed satisfactory performance in real water samples, with results in good agreement with those obtained by ICP-MS85. A recent study described a nanoporous gold (NPG)-modified screen-printed carbon electrode for the concurrent determination of Pb(II) and Cu(II) using ASV. The device achieved linear response intervals of 1–100 µg/L for Pb(II) and 10–100 µg/L for Cu(II), with corresponding detection limits of 0.4 µg/L and 5.4 µg/L (S/N ratio of 3 method) in simulated deionized water, indicating its reliability for monitoring these metals in drinking water. Measurements in real water samples were in good agreement with those obtained by ICP-MS86. Enhancing the performance of gold-based electrochemical platforms, one study described an IoT-integrated sensor that employs gold nanoparticle-functionalized carbon thread electrodes for multiplexed detection of Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II). Using DPV, the sensor achieved LOD (3σ method) of 111 µg/L, 128 µg/L, 87.6 µg/L, and 144 µg/L for Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II), respectively, in simulated deionized water. The approach was validated by examining real lake water samples using the standard addition approach47. A sophisticated study introduced a microfluidic multichannel electrochemical cell engineered for the simultaneous monitoring of multiple metal ions. In this configuration, a bare gold electrode was used to directly sense Hg(II), achieving a detection limit of 0.9 µg/L (3σ method) in spiked ultrapure water, while a gold electrode modified with a thin mercury film was employed for Pb(II), attaining an LOD as low as 0.1 µg/L. Application of this device to industrial effluents and river water demonstrated excellent concordance with ICP-OES measurements87. The primary weakness of gold electrodes is their cost and poor resistance to fouling in complex matrices. This high material cost drives two key design strategies to improve translational feasibility: (1) using gold as a thin-film surface modifier on a low-cost, disposable substrate like an SPCE to reduce the total precious metal usage; and (2) integration into highly controlled microfluidic systems to manage sample matrix complexity and extend sensor life. The scalability of nanostructured gold electrodes via controlled electrodeposition is feasible, but maintaining high batch-to-batch reproducibility of complex nanocomposite interfaces remains a significant challenge. Bismuth-film and antimony-film electrodes represent a direct interface engineering strategy to replace mercury: a non-toxic metal film is plated in-situ or ex-situ onto a carbon substrate. Their performance is a direct consequence of mimicking mercury's ability to form alloys, but with distinct property trade-offs that dictate their optimal protocol selection. Bismuth film electrodes represent a promising advancement in electrochemical methodologies, offering attributes such as simple fabrication, heightened sensitivity, distinct peak resolution, tolerance to dissolved oxygen in aqueous phases, limited toxicity, and operational capability under strong alkaline conditions88,89. The use of bismuth is considered a viable and environmentally friendly alternative to mercury, which has traditionally been utilized in ASV for detecting heavy metals such as Cu(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), and Zn(II). For example, research reported the fabrication of a Nafion/bismuth nanoparticle-modified screen-printed graphene electrode, which they applied to the anodic stripping voltammetric detection of Pb(II) and Cd(II)90. The sensor achieved LOD of 0.28 µg/L for Pb(II) and 40.34 µg/L for Cd(II), confirming its effectiveness for on-site monitoring of environmental samples such as treated wastewater and bay water. The results were also benchmarked against those obtained by AAS, demonstrating good agreement. In a distinct methodological direction, a study reported the fabrication of a bismuth-modified porous graphene heterostructure for the ultrasensitive determination of Cd(II)91. By coupling the bicontinuous porous network of bismuth with the superior electrical conductivity of graphene, the resulting sensor delivered a linear range of 0.11–1.12 µg/L and an exceptionally low detection limit of 0.011 µg/L(S/N ratio of 3 method) for Cd(II) in synthetic ultrapure water, highlighting the efficacy of nanostructured designs91. A further notable trend is the use of biopolymers for constructing eco-friendly sensing platforms. In this context, research developed a screen-printed carbon electrode modified with a bismuth-chitosan nanocomposite for the concurrent quantification of several metal ions92. The presence of chitosan, a biocompatible polymer, significantly enhanced the analytical response. Under optimized conditions, the device achieved LODs (3σ method) of 0.1 µg/L for Zn(II), 0.1 µg/L for Cd(II), and 0.2 µg/L for Pb(II) in synthetic deionized water. Applied to real wastewater collected from a mining operation and benchmarked against ICP-MS, the method yielded LOD of 0.4 µg/L for Cd(II) and 0.3 µg/L for Pb(II). The sensor showed high precision, with relative standard deviations ranging from 1.3% to 5.6%92. Innovation in electrochemical monitoring now extends beyond the composition of the sensing material to include sophisticated electrode design and operational methodology. An anonymous researcher reported the use of a bismuth film-modified glassy carbon rotating disk electrode for concurrent detection of Pb(II) and Cu(II) in Danube River samples using the standard addition method. Optimization of the experimental conditions, particularly the incorporation of EDTA as a complexing ligand and fine-tuning of the rotation speed, enabled the attainment of competitive detection limits (σ method) in synthetic ultrapure water of approximately 4.29 µg/L for Pb(II) and 0.67 µg/L for Cu(II)93. The introduction of antimony film-modified electrodes has marked an important step forward in electrochemical heavy metal monitoring in wastewater, serving as a greener alternative to traditional mercury-based systems. Owing to their capacity to operate under more acidic conditions and their reduced toxicological impact, antimony film electrodes (SbFEs) conform well to the ethos of green analytical chemistry94,95. Typically, these electrodes function by electroplating an antimony film, in situ or ex situ, onto a carbon-based substrate such as a glassy carbon electrode or a screen-printed carbon electrode. After modification, the electrode preconcentrates heavy metal ions via reduction, which are subsequently reoxidized during an ASV scan, giving rise to current intensities proportional to their concentration. The quality of the electroanalytical response is critically influenced by both the type of carbon substrate and the morphology of the antimony film, prompting sustained efforts to design new nanocomposite materials that provide superior sensitivity and long-term stability95,96. A sequential injection-ASV approach employing an in-situ deposited antimony film on a GO-modified screen-printed carbon electrode (SbF-GO-SPCE) exhibited outstanding analytical performance for the concurrent quantification of several heavy metals. Linear calibration ranges of 0.1–1.5 µmol L−1 were established for Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II), with LOD (3σ method) of approximately 6.1 µg/L, 5.4 µg/L, 3.8 µg/L, and 13.2 µg/L in synthetic ultrapure water, respectively, thereby demonstrating the system's enhanced capability for multi-element detection97,98. The electrode was successfully applied to determine these metals in real wastewater samples collected from sewage, fertilizer effluents, and seawater, and the results were compared with those obtained by AAS. A particularly noteworthy approach involved fabricating a reduced GO-antimony nanoparticles (rGO-Sb NPs) nanocomposite on a glassy carbon substrate. This electrochemical sensor capitalized on the excellent charge-transport performance of rGO in combination with the high specific surface area and catalytic efficiency of Sb NPs. The method provided very low detection limits (3σ method) in synthetic purified water: 5.1 µg/L for Cd(II), 3.4 µg/L for Pb(II), 2.2 µg/L for Cu(II), and 0.96 µg/L for Hg(II), indicating a substantial gain in sensitivity, particularly for Pb(II) and Hg(II) determination96. An additional innovative strategy was described that involved the application of two-dimensional (2D) antimonene. A Sb-SPCE was effectively employed for the simultaneous voltammetric determination of Pb(II) and Cd(II) using DPV. The sensor exhibited a linear response for Pb(II) in the range 1.1–128.3 µg/L with LOD (3σ method) of 0.3 µg/L, and for Cd(II) in the range 9.1–132.7 µg/L with a detection limit of 2.7 µg/L in spiked purified water. These results demonstrate the feasibility of using antimony allotropes themselves as the electrode modifier for trace analysis in real matrices such as tap water, providing performance comparable to that of ICP-MS99. This class presents a significant trade-off between analytical merit and operational stability. Their low toxicity, compatibility, and low cost with SPCEs grant them excellent field scalability, deployability, and cost-effectiveness90,92. However, their major analytical limitation is their long-term stability and batch reproducibility. The morphology of the electroplated film is critically dependent on deposition parameters (time, potential, solution composition) and the underlying substrate roughness. This leads to film leaching during stripping cycles and poor sensor-to-sensor reproducibility, a problem well-documented for mercury films that is inherited by these analogous systems. Electroanalytical chemistry has experienced a notable shift regarding the detection of heavy metal ions (HMIs) in water, greatly accelerated by the introduction of advanced nanomaterials. These materials overcome the inherent drawbacks of traditional electrodes, including slow electron transfer rates, small active surface area, and a higher chance of surface fouling. Among the diverse classes of emerging materials, carbon-based nanomaterials, MOF, metal NPs, and conducting polymers have gained remarkable attention due to their outstanding physicochemical properties, including high electrical conductivity, high surface area, chemical stability, and facile surface modification. Figure 5 maps the classification and taxonomy of advanced nanomaterials engineered to functionalize electrochemical interfaces for trace heavy metal detection. Schematic classification and key properties of advanced nanomaterials employed to modify electrochemical interfaces for heavy metal detection, including metal and metal oxide nanoparticles (trade-off: high cost for noble metals), carbonaceous materials, polymeric materials (trade-off: limited mechanical stability and possible film swelling), QD (trade-off: moderate sensitivity unless combined with metals), and MOFs/COFs; trade-off: poor intrinsic conductivity requiring conductive composites). Performance note: no single class optimizes all analytical figures of merit; hybrid composites (e.g., MOF/CNT, Bi/rGO, Au/QDs) better balance sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and cost for field-deployable sensors. Original image by authors. Carbon-based nanomaterials, from 0D QD to 2D graphene sheets, form cutting-edge platforms for voltammetric sensing. Their principal function is to amplify the electrochemically active area and promote efficient electron exchange at the electrode–electrolyte interface, thereby markedly reduced LOD for hazardous metals such as Pb, Cd, Hg, As, and Cu100. Graphene, composed of a single atomic layer of \(s{p}^{2}\) bonded carbon, and its derivatives rGO and GO, have emerged as a benchmark material in high-performance electrochemical sensing platforms. The exceptional carrier mobility coupled with extensive theoretical surface area (\(2600\,{{\rm{m}}}^{2}\,{{\rm{g}}}^{-1}\)) of graphene serves as an efficient substrate for the accumulation of metal ions throughout the preconcentration stage of stripping voltammetry101. Recent research underscores the utility of rGO, that restored \(s{p}^{2}\) carbon lattice and promotes conductivity compared to GO. As a case in point, an rGO/Bi nanocomposite electrode enabled trace detection of Pb(II) and Cd(II) in tap water using SWV, affording LOD of 0.46 and 0.66 µg/L in spiked ultrapure water, respectively102. Similarly, the Bi nanoparticle/laser-induced graphene sensor exhibited nearly identical limits of detection (3σ method, 0.5 and 0.8 µg/L) in synthetic deionized water, underscoring that the combined effect of the high-surface-area rGO framework and the mercury-like properties of Bi promotes alloy formation and enhances peak current responses103,104. These two studies exemplify a crucial design principle: the rGO scaffold provides the conductive and high-area platform, but the selectivity and preconcentration efficiency are imparted by the Bi component, which mimics mercury via amalgam formation. The trade-off is that the long-term stability of such composite films under repeated stripping cycles is rarely reported; gradual Bi leaching and rGO restacking can degrade performance over time, a gap that must be addressed for continuous monitoring applications. Furthermore, Ag-functionalized rGO enabled sensitive As(III) detection via DPV, achieving a 0.24 µg/L LOD in spiked purified water using the 3σ method, with performance that showed consistent reproducibility and reliability in real water samples analyzed by the standard addition method105. This real‑sample validation using standard addition strengthens confidence in matrix robustness, though long‑term fouling data in organic‑rich natural waters remain absent. The introduction of ruthenium into the GO framework enabled electrochemical sensing of As(III), Cd(II), Hg(II), and Pb(II) by DPV, with LODs in simulated purified water of 0.17, 0.31, 0.32, and 0.29 µg/L, respectively. This system achieved simultaneous determination of these heavy metals in river and tap water samples, with the results validated by AAS106. The Ru/GO sensor is notable for its multi-element capability and successful cross-validation with AAS, yet the operational stability over 60days showed a marked decline for mercury (retained 73.8%), highlighting that even high-performance graphene-based sensors suffer from gradual signal degradation for certain analytes, a critical consideration for field deployment where recalibration is impractical. Another graphene/dimethylglyoxime/Nafion-modified GCE was employed for the selective determination of Ni(II) ions in water samples via adsorptive stripping voltammetry in the presence of common interfering ions such as Co(II) and Zn(II). It demonstrated good selectivity, achieving a LOD (3σ method) of 1.5 µg/L in spiked ultrapure distilled water, which complies with drinking-water guideline levels107. Selectivity here is achieved via a dual mechanism: dimethylglyoxime provides specific Ni(II) chelation while the Nafion film electrostatically excludes anionic interferents-an elegant example of interfacial engineering that requires precise control over film thickness to maintain reproducibility across electrodes. A QDs/GO composite integrated on a GCE functions as an ASV platform capable of detecting Cd(II) and Pb(II) simultaneously, exploiting abundant oxygen groups and a large surface area to facilitate electrostatic accumulation of the metal species. The homogeneous dispersion of QDs enhances the metal re-oxidation kinetics during the stripping step, resulting in improved analytical performance compared with QDs/GCE and GO/GCE electrodes. This is evidenced by wide detection ranges and limits of detection (3σ method) for Cd(II) of 11.24–11241 μg/L with an LOD of 7.45 μg/L, and for Pb(II) of 20.72–10360 μg/L with an LOD of 1.17 μg/L, along with consistent recoveries in real water samples determined using the standard addition method108. Combining \(0\)D QD with \(2\)D GO can enhance surface area, conductivity, and binding-site density. However, the scalability of such composites remains constrained by the need for precise control over QD size, interfacial coupling, and loading uniformity. Overall, graphene-based electrodes can achieve among the lowest reported LODs, and their selectivity is readily tuned through functionalization. Yet, literature is still dominated by short-term measurements in spiked samples. Quantitative assessments of fouling resistance in humic-acid-rich matrices, long-term (beyond several months) signal stability, and batch-to-batch reproducibility of drop-cast graphene films remain scarce. MWCNTs, and single-walled (SWCNTs) are known for their high aspect ratio, electrical conductivity, surface area, excellent electron transfer, high transduction capacity, and remarkable mechanical strength. In the context of voltammetry, CNTs serve as “molecular wires”, facilitating electron transfer between redox species and the electrode surface109. An illustrative example is the modification of a GCE with an amino clay-based polymer, featuring MWCNTs to boost conductivity for the detection of Cu(II), Hg(II), and Pb(II) via the DPV technique. With the MWCNT network, rapid signal transduction is achieved, enabling excellent sensitivity in complex matrices. Under these conditions, LOD (S/N ratio of 3 method) of 1.0 × 10⁻⁴, 6.0 × 10⁻⁵, and 1.2 × 10⁻⁴ µg/L were obtained, with corresponding linear ranges of 3.18 × 10⁻⁴–6.36 × 101 µg/L, 2.01 × 10⁻⁴–2.01 × 102 µg/L, and 4.14 × 10⁻⁴–2.07 × 102 µg/L for Cu(II), Hg(II), and Pb(II), respectively110. CNTs, acting as conductive conduits within a chelating polymer matrix, enable very low LODs by markedly amplifying the faradaic response; however, such ultra-trace detection is often coupled to relatively narrow linear ranges, a trade-off that must be weighed against the concentration window of the intended application. ZnO NPs decorated with MWCNT electrodes enabled simultaneous and sensitive electrochemical quantification of Cu(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) in water samples by SWV using the spike recovery method. Linear ranges of 1–800 µg/L, 5–1000 µg/L, and 1–900 µg/L were achieved, with LOD (3σ method) of 0.8, 1.2, and 0.7 ppb for Cu(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II), respectively, determined in simulated deionized water. The electrode demonstrated approximately three to five times the sensitivity compared with the unmodified counterpart, accompanied by sharp, well-resolved peaks and negligible overlap from Co(II), Mn(II), and Fe(III), yielding recoveries ranging from 95% to 102%111. However, the lack of data on signal decay under repeated use or in the presence of organic foulants means that their field-readiness remains only partially established. Furthermore, Zn(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II) were simultaneously detected by SWV using a GCE functionalized with a Fe3O4/fluorinated MWCNT nanocomposite, delivering clear peak profiles with enhanced sensitivity. Optimized electrochemical parameters yielded LOD (3σ method) of 1.57 µg/L for Cd(II), 1.74 µg/L for Pb(II), 0.78 µg/L for Hg(II), 0.78 µg/L for Zn(II), and 0.34 µg/L for Cu(II) in spiked purified water. The sensor remained stable for at least 30 days, a performance attributed to the negatively polarized C–F groups, the conductive architecture, and the synergistic interaction between Fe3O4 and CNTs112. The 30-day stability data represent a valuable reference point for long-term performance reporting and provide a useful template that future studies across different nanomaterial platforms should aim to match or exceed. Compared with graphene, CNT-based sensors can deliver similar levels of signal amplification, yet uniform dispersion and reproducible film morphology are typically more difficult to achieve. As zero-dimensional nanomaterials typically below 10 nm, carbon and graphene QDs are increasingly employed as functional modifiers, owing to their high surface functionality and compatibility with biological environments. QDs have been effectively applied in environmental fields, drug delivery, light-emitting technologies, biosensing, anti-counterfeiting, water monitoring, and food analysis113. Compared with their bulk equivalents, QDs exhibit tunable electronic and optical characteristics controlled through heteroatom doping (N, S, B, P, or Se)114. For example, Heteroatoms N, O, and S-doped carbon QDs were used to identify and quantify copper in water samples113. Furthermore, N-doped graphene QDs showed superior selectivity toward Hg(II) over a broad range of competing metal ions, including Cd(II), Zn(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Ba(II), Mn(II), Pb(II), Cr(III), Al(III), and Fe(III). Under these conditions, a LOD of 10 µg/L for Hg(II) was achieved in synthetic ultrapure water using the S/N ratio of 3 method115. The strong Hg(II) selectivity of the sensor across such a broad ion panel illustrates how heteroatom doping can create favorable coordination sites; however, the LOD of 10 µg/L remains modest compared with gold- or bismuth-based sensors, underscoring that selectivity and sensitivity do not necessarily track and must be balanced according to the intended application. Notably, the literature documents that Graphene QDs doped Au NPs were successfully detected Cu and Hg by ASV, with exceptionally low LODs (S/N ratio method) of 3.2 × 10−3, 4.0 × 10−3 µg/L, respectively116. The synergy between QDs and Au NPs, where QDs increase the density of binding sites and Au NPs promote amalgam formation and facilitate electron transfer, illustrates how hybridization can drive LODs into the sub-ppb regime, although the cost and fouling susceptibility of the gold component must be carefully weighed in any field-deployment scenario. Furthermore, a recent study reported that a graphene QDs /porphyrin composite can act as a sensor for monitoring Cd(II) and Cu(II) ions in seawater by SWV without any pre-treatment, achieving LOD of 49.0 and 10.9 µg/L, respectively, in simulated purified water using the 3σ method117. The ability to detect seawater without pre-treatment is a significant step toward field deployability, but the LODs are above many regulatory guideline values, revealing a remaining gap between operational simplicity and analytical sensitivity in complex saline matrices. Zn(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II) were detected by a nanocomposite of graphene QDs and chitosan via SWV without interfering with K, Na, and Al(III) ions, with LODs 8.83, 3.11, and 1.99 µg/L118. Graphene QDs decorated graphene functioned as a simple yet effective ASV platform for Cu(II) determination in real water samples, providing results comparable to those obtained by AAS. It afforded a linear response from 0.95 to 557 µg/L and a detection limit (S/N = 3) of 0.083 µg/L in simulated deionized water, with negligible interference from a wide range of organic molecules CHCl3, CHBr3, C6H5Cl, C6Cl5OH, CH2Cl2 and metal ions including Zn(II), Cd(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Hg(II), Mn(II), Pb(II), Fe(III), Al(III), Na, K, and Sn(II)119. The interference study testing metal ions at 15-fold and organic substances at 100-fold excess relative to Cu(II) is commendably comprehensive and sets a useful standard for selectivity evaluation; the high tolerance under such conditions supports strong anti-fouling performance against these species, although direct comparison with their typical environmental concentrations would further strengthen the field-relevance assessment. CNFs constitute a class of one-dimensional nanomaterials characterized by a stacked-cup or platelet arrangement, facilitating the exposure of abundant accessible edge-plane sites120. These sites are electrochemically more active than the basal planes found in CNTs or graphene. The electrochemical sensing capabilities of CNFs are attributable to their large surface area, chemically tunable surface functionality, high electrical conductivity, structural stability, and compatibility with multi-material integration120,121,122. Compared with CNTs, CNFs often present a higher proportion of edge-plane-like sites per unit mass, which can confer favorable heterogeneous electron-transfer kinetics even in the absence of extensive post-synthesis doping or functionalization. For instance, CNFs decorated by CuO were utilized to detect three different classes of contaminants in real tap water samples, including heavy metals, antibiotics, and pesticides (tylosin tartrate, chloramphenicol, imidacloprid, quinalphos, lead, and cadmium) via DPV123. The combination of bis(indolyl)methane and mesoporous CNFs on a GCE has been utilized as an ASV sensor for Hg(II) quantification in river water, providing results comparable to those obtained by AAS. It delivers excellent selectivity and sensitivity in multi-metal matrices containing Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cu(II), achieving a detection limit of 0.06 µg/L in simulated purified water using the 3σ method124. The sub-ppb LOD positions CNF-based sensors competitively against Au-electrode systems, with the added advantages of lower material cost and excellent storage stability (92% signal retention after four weeks); however, the long-term operational stability under repeated oxidative stripping cycles, a prerequisite for reusable field sensors, is not reported, leaving a gap in the assessment of practical usability. Another reported sensor design combines bimetallic PtAu with CNFs for the simultaneous SWASV detection of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cu(II), achieving a lowest detected concentration (corresponding to 11.2 µg/L, 20.7 µg/L, and 6.4 µg/L) with relative standard deviations of 4-8%125. The use of PtAu NPs introduces a clear cost burden; while the sensor offers good repeatability and simultaneous multi-element detection, the absence of cost-normalized benchmarking against monometallic or metal-free CNF systems leaves the economic case for such noble-metal hybrids in routine monitoring unresolved. Vertically aligned CNFs have also been applied for Pb(II) determination by ASV, achieving a LOD of 0.35 µg/L based on the 3σ method, highlighting their strong capability for trace lead analysis in aqueous samples126. The application of a carbon paste electrode incorporating a polypyrrole/CNF nanocomposite enabled trace detection of Pb(II) in real water samples, with a detection limit of 0.05 µg/L (3σ method) determined in spiked purified water and excellent linearity across the tested range127. A recent publication presented a screen-printed sensor architecture featuring CNFs functionalized with Fe3O4 NPs alongside MWCNT bearing Fe3O4 NPs to simultaneously detect Cd(II), Pb(II), Hg(II), and Cu(II), where the Fe3O4/CNF modified sensor outperformed the Fe3O4/ MWCNT variant in terms of detection limit (for example, 3.2 and 66 µg/L for Pb(II)), respectively128. This direct head-to-head comparison provides evidence that CNFs can outperform MWCNTs in electrochemical sensing, which the authors link to more uniform Fe3O4 dispersion on the CNF scaffold, improved structural stability, and a more favorable synergy between components. Integrating metal and metal oxide nanostructures into electrochemical platforms has advanced the sensitivity and selectivity of voltammetric heavy metal measurements, addressing their relevance in environmental monitoring and exposure control. These nanostructured materials exhibit markedly enhanced catalytic activity together with a higher electroactive surface area (EASA) than bulk counterparts, thereby accelerating electron-transfer reactions and facilitating the detection of heavy metal ions at ultra-trace levels, spanning from parts per billion to parts per trillion129. Noble and transition metal NPs serve widely as electrode modifiers, attributable to their superior conductive properties and their ability to form alloys or amalgams with heavy metal ions, which is critically important in stripping voltammetric measurements130. As an illustration, recent work has shown that bismuth nanoplates and bismuth/Nafion composites can detect Pb(II) and Cd(II) via ASV with LOD (3.3σ method) of approximately 0.037 and 0.042 µg/L, respectively, obtained under optimized conditions in an acetate buffer that simulates ultrapure water131. These sensors also enabled low-nanomolar Pb(II) and Cd(II) determination in spiked tap water (LODs ≈0.043 and 0.46 µg/L) and in industrial effluent (LODs ≈ 1.19 and 3.8 µg/L), confirming their practical relevance for real-sample analysis. Additionally, the sensor showed good selectivity toward Pb(II) and Cd(II) in the presence of multiple inorganics and organic interferents, 14-day stability with <10% signal variation, and batch-to-batch reproducibility of about 6–8% for n = 6. It's simple drop-casting/dipping fabrication, compatibility with portable potentiostats, and Nafion-based antifouling and cation-exchange properties support low-cost field deployability. Bismuth modified screen printed electrodes, and GCE enabled cathodic stripping voltammetric analysis of Rh, Pt, and Pd, reaching LOD of 0.005, 0.006, and 0.008 μg/L, respectively, in standard solutions and applied in water and sediment samples without interfering with Fe(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Na, SO42−, and phosphate PO43− 132. Selectivity is good for common ions, whereas Co(II) and Ni(II) significantly affect the signal when present at equal or higher concentrations, a limitation not observed for the Bi/Nafion Cd/Pb system. Stability was verified over 28 h with minimal loss of peak current, but longer-term data is missing. Reproducibility (RSD 5–9%) can be regarded as moderate. Additionally, a porous Au electrode prepared on GCE using an Au-based Prussian blue analog sacrificial template has been employed for the detection of trace As(III) in water, with ASV and the standard addition method affording a LOD of 0.49 µg/L (S/N = 3) for the As(0)→As(III) peak in acidified solutions133. This electrode provides good real-sample performance in tap, spring, and river water, with recoveries of approximately 99–103%, 98.6–104%, and 98.2–101%, respectively, and associated RSDs of about 2.6–2.9%, 3.2–4.1%, and 3.4–3.7%. Long-term stability over 30 days (RSD < 4%) and inter-electrode reproducibility around 3% are excellent. However, selectivity can be compromised by Cu(II), which overlaps the As(0) → As(III) signal in 0.5 M H₂SO4; this interference can be mitigated by exploiting the second oxidation peak (As(III) → As(V)) or by operating in phosphate buffer, a clever workaround but still a practical constraint. Moreover, the fabrication requires co-electrodeposition of Au and the Prussian blue analog, followed by alkali/acid template removal, making it more complex and potentially more costly than simple drop-casting routes typically used for Bi- or carbon-based sensors, and thus less attractive for large-scale, low-cost production. Another example of Au NPs/GCE for quantifying As(III) with LOD (S/N ratio of 3 method) of 1.8 μg/L in a laboratory electrolyte that effectively simulates ultrapure water conditions134. This sensor is less sensitive than the porous Au electrode described above and, in the available report, lacks validation in real water matrices, long-term stability testing, and batch-to-batch reproducibility data. Fabrication by electrodeposition of Au nanoparticles in the presence of iodide is relatively straightforward, but the absence of real-sample evaluation means that fouling resistance and field deployability remain uncertain, so overall this system is less comprehensively characterized than other As(III) sensors in this section. Furthermore, Se(IV) and As(III) were simultaneously determined in water using an AuNP-modified GCE by ASV, achieving LODs of 0.22 and 0.15 µg/L, respectively (3σ method), in deionized water and validated against ICP-OES measurements using a t-test at the 95% confidence level135. This sensor offers excellent sub µg/L sensitivity for dual-analyte detection and shows good repeatability, with RSDs of 3.2% and 2.8% for Se(IV) and As(III), respectively. Selectivity was evaluated against common cations, with negligible effects from Mg2⁺, Ca2⁺, Na⁺, and K⁺ and only minimal interference from Cu(II) and Cd(II), causing about 6.25% peak enhancement and 2.3% peak suppression, respectively. Fabrication by simple electrodeposition of AuNPs onto GCE is straightforward and low-cost, and the successful agreement with ICP-OES for industrial effluent samples (5.23 vs 5.60 µg/L for As(III) and 5.16 vs 3.7 µg/L for Se(IV)) suggests good potential for on-site monitoring, although long-term stability and fouling resistance in more complex matrices were not explicitly investigated. The Pt NPs-modified electrode served as a simple yet effective ASV sensor for As(III), affording a linear response from 40 to 7950 nM and a sensitivity of 0.356 mA ppb−1, with negligible interference from a comprehensive set of metal ions, particularly Cu(II), which showed minimal stripping signal at the As(III) oxidation potential136. Another representative noble metal system involves Pd NPs impregnated CPE for Hg(II) sensing, which achieves a LOD of 1.00 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water, a value compatible with US EPA drinking water standards. This sensor has been successfully applied to tap water and municipal wastewater samples using the standard addition method137. In another design, a PANT/green AgNP modified CPE enabled simultaneous Cd(II) and Pb(II) determination by SWV, with LODs of 0.09 and 0.05 µg/L (S/N = 3) in acetate buffer. The sensor combines sub-ppb sensitivity, acceptable seawater recoveries (92–114%), good stability (>92% signal retention over 14 days), and reproducible fabrication (RSD < 4.2%), while remaining low-cost and scalable, though long-term field robustness and fouling behavior were not fully assessed138. A GCE modified by casting Au/Pd NPs onto its surface has been employed for the ASV detection of As(III), achieving an LOD of 0.25 µg/L (S/N‑based) in 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 4.5), with negligible interference from Cd(II), Pb(II), Zn(II), and Mn(II)139. The Au–Pd/GCE sensor, therefore, offers good sensitivity and selectivity toward several co‑metal ions, but no real‑sample analysis is reported, which is a major gap for assessing performance in complex matrices. Cu(II) and Hg(II) cause significant interference, which can be alleviated by adding sulfate (0.06–0.08 M), and stability was only evaluated over 20 consecutive cycles without signal loss, with no information on long‑term storage stability or batch‑to‑batch reproducibility. Fabrication by casting pre‑synthesized Au–Pd core shell NPs is of moderate complexity and higher material cost (use of both Au and Pd), and in the absence of real‑sample validation, field deployability remains uncertain. In a separate configuration, bimetallic Au/Pt were employed for the quantification of As(III) by ASV, in 0.5 M H₂SO4, giving LODs of 0.28 and 0.45 µg/L (S/N = 3) for the As(0) → As(III) and As(III) → As(V) peaks over linear ranges of 0.37–225 and 0.75–225 µg/L, respectively140. The design exploits mild hydrogen evolution at Pt sites to chemically reduce As(III) to As(0), enhancing cathodic preconcentration and yielding sharp dual stripping signals at neighboring Au sites. Real water analysis is reported, but the excerpt does not detail recoveries, long-term stability, or electrode-to-electrode reproducibility, so durability and batch scalability remain unclear. Fabrication by short-time co-electrodeposition of Au–Pt NPs is straightforward, though noble-metal cost (Au + Pt) is higher than for single-metal or non-noble systems. Au/Bi bimetallic nanoparticle-functionalized GO nanocomposites were fabricated and applied for the selective quantification of Fe(III) in seawater and lake samples using the standard addition method. The sensor exhibited a linear response over 11.2–2,790 µg/L with a LOD of 3.9 µg/L (S/N = 3) determined in in 0.1 M HCl, without significant interference from K, Na, Pb(II), Zn(II), Mn(II), Ca, Cu(II), Al(III), or Mg ions, and was successfully applied to lake and seawater, with recoveries of ~90–103% and RSD < 2.7% when validated against ICP-MS141. The family of metal oxides, including TiO2, Fe3O4, Fe2O3, CuO, ZnO, SnO2, and CeO2, represents another core class of nanoparticle modifiers for heavy metal sensing platforms142,143. Nanoparticles exhibit several practical benefits, notably magnetic behavior that simplifies electrode modification with high chemical stability and plentiful surface hydroxyl functionalities for analyte binding144,145. For instance, Fe3O4 NPs were used to detect Hg(II), Cu(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II) via ASV in river water samples, with stability over 15 cycles, giving RSD 4.9%, and spiked river water recovery was 91.5%146. Fe₂O3 nanorods and hollow nanocubes (both derived from Prussian blue) were compared as GCE modifiers for ASV detection of Pb(II) in aqueous solution. Despite the hollow nanocubes having a larger BET surface area, Fe₂O3 nanorods exposing the (010) plane delivered much higher sensitivity (109.67 µA mM−1) and a lower LOD of 0.70 µg/L than the hollow nanocubes (17.68 µA mM−1, LOD 17.2 µg/L), underscoring that specific adsorption sites (Fe–O) dominate over total surface area in governing electroanalytical performance. The nanorod-modified GCE was successfully applied to spiked real water samples, giving Pb(II) recoveries of 92–108% with RSD ≤ 8.4%147. Fabrication (etching + calcination) is moderately complex but uses low-cost precursors. Scalability is reasonable, though the multi-step synthesis increases cost relative to drop-cast nanoparticles. In another recent study, TiO2 Nanorods were used for the simultaneous identification of Cr(III), Hg(II), and Cu(II) ions with notable high sensitivity of 15.6, 19.7and 92.2 µAmM−1 cm−2 and LODs of about 1.13 × 106, 5.72 × 106, and 2.35 × 106 µg/L, respectively143. The TiO₂ electrode shows good reliability (small current variation across five electrodes) and retains ~92% of its initial Cu2⁺ sensitivity over 30 days, but its relatively high LODs restrict use to relatively contaminated samples rather than ultra-trace monitoring. ZnO nanofiber-coated GCE has been applied as a sensing platform for Cd(II) determination with SWV, with a linear response range between 0.54 and 146 µg/L and LOD (S/N method) of 0.20 µg/L that was determined in 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 4.6). The electrode shows markedly higher Cd(II) stripping currents than bare, Nafion-coated, or ZnO NP modified GCEs, consistent with enhanced adsorption via the high-area nanofiber network and dense surface hydroxyl groups, and was successfully applied to Cd determination in real water samples, with simultaneous multi-metal (Cd, Pb, Cu, Hg) analysis also demonstrated148. In separate configurations, a CeO2 NPs fabricated via a simple sucrose/nitrate combustion route was used for ASV quantification of Zn(II) in 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 6), giving a linear range of 20–380 µg/L and an LOD of 0.36 µg/L. The CeO₂ film (10–12 nm particles, mesoporous, ~18 m2/g) greatly enhances Zn(II) oxidation currents relative to bare GCE through increased active area and adsorption, and the sensor shows good selectivity against common cations/anions, with real industrial effluents (battery and metal processing wastewaters) yielding recoveries near 99–101% and RSD < 4%149. Fabrication (combustion synthesis followed by drop-casting) is simple, low-cost, and in principle scalable. The straightforward modification step supports good potential for field deployment, although actual on-site trials are not reported. Fouling resistance was not explicitly investigated. Nanocomposite of SnO2/rGO doped with Co/Fe was used for selective detection of Cr (VI) and Cd (II) ions via CV, giving linear responses from about 0.07 to 50 µg/L with LODs of 0.07 and 0.04 µg/L detected in simulated high-quality deionized water, respectively, well below WHO guideline values150. The doped SnO₂/rGO architectures significantly enhance sensitivity (up to ~3.2 µA/µg·L−1 for Cd and ~9.4 µA/µg·L−1 for Cr) relative to bare SnO₂ or rGO, while maintaining good selectivity against most common ions and retaining ~92% of the initial signal after 30 days of storage. Real-matrix testing is limited to prepared aqueous solutions, leaving a major gap in evaluating matrix effects. Interference studies show moderate signal perturbation from Hg(II) and As(III) in Cr(VI) sensing and from Pb(II) in Cd(II) sensing, whereas Na(I), K(I), Mn(II), Zn(II), Mg(II), Ca(II), and Al(III) have negligible impact. Fabrication via hydrothermal synthesis plus ultrasonication is multi-step, though it uses low-cost precursors, giving moderate scalability. Another study reported the detection of Cd(II), Hg(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II) ions using a 3D interlayer rGO/SnO₂/polypyrrole nanohybrid (rGO from expanded graphite, SnO₂ nanoparticles, polypyrrole overlayer) via ASV. The method exhibited excellent sensitivity and current density, with LODs (3σ method) of 8.4 × 10⁻⁵, 1.6 × 10⁻⁴, 5.3 × 10⁻⁵, 1.6 × 10⁻⁴, and 1.8 × 10⁻⁴ µg/L that were detected in simulated high-quality deionized water, respectively151. The study reports no real-sample tests, leaving matrix effects and practical performance unassessed. Interference data for this specific hybrid, as well as long-term stability and batch-to-batch reproducibility, are all absent. Fabrication by hydrothermal synthesis followed by in-situ polymerization is relatively complex and multi-step, which may hinder scale-up. Simultaneous detection of trace Tl(I) and Hg(II) in water samples by SnO2/MWCNTs Modified GCE via ASV with a limit of quantification (3σ method, 3.0 × 10−3 and 4.0 × 10−3 µg/L) and detection ranged from 0.003 to 450 and 0.004 to 400 µg/L, respectively152. The sensor shows low RSDs (≈2–4%) for repeatability/reproducibility, retains its response over three weeks, and delivers 96.7–99.1% recoveries in spiked surface waters, with results consistent with ICP-MS. Fabrication (casting of nanocomposite) is straightforward and relatively low-cost, as it uses commercially available MWCNTs. The simple platform, ng/L level LODs, multiweek stability, and successful surface water measurements indicate good potential for field deployment, though dedicated on-site trials are not reported. Fouling resistance was not directly evaluated, but stable currents and accurate recoveries in surface waters imply acceptable matrix tolerance. As a new generation of functional materials, MOFs and COFs have recently been recognized as advanced platforms for voltammetric heavy-metal ion detection, owing to their remarkable architectural diversity and finely adjustable functional sites. MOFs possess crystalline porous frameworks constructed from metal ions or clusters bridged by organic linkers, providing both abundant active sites and a significant internal surface area, facilitating the accumulation of analytes153,154. Likewise, COFs are crystalline porous frameworks formed from light elements (C, H, N, O, B) connected via strong covalent interactions, delivering exceptional chemical durability and well-defined pore size and structural geometry155. A broad range of COF materials, including triazine- and imine-linked architectures, feature an ordered porous network that facilitates selective diffusion of metal ions, while their inherently modular design permits the installation of functional groups (amino, thiol, carboxyl, etc.) to enhance coordination efficiency and selectivity156,157. Furthermore, MOF- and COF-derived composites such as carbonaceous materials and metal /metal oxide NPs hybrids harness beneficial structural features from the parent frameworks that translate into enhanced electrical conductivity and catalytic behavior, and their synergistic coupling with conductive nanomaterials or supports resolves the limitation of poor native conductivity of the pristine frameworks and improves rapid charge transfer at the electrode interface153,154,155,156,157. Consequently, these frameworks serve simultaneously as pre-concentrating adsorbents and as active sensing phases, thereby collectively enhancing voltammetric sensitivity and selectivity153,154. Recent work demonstrated that the UiO-66-NH2(Zr)/GO nanocomposite drop-cast on GCE (Nafion binder) has been successfully demonstrated as a selective voltammetric probe for Cu(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II) via ASV, affording LODs (3σ method) of 0.59, 2.9, and 0.84 µg/L in an acetate buffer (pH 4.5)158. The MOF/GO hybrid (one-pot hydrothermal growth of UiO-66-NH₂ on GO) provides high sensitivity of 1.30 µA µM−1 for Cu(II), 0.50 µA µM−1 for Cd(II), and 12.38 µA µM−1 for Pb(II), and the sensor was successfully applied to lake, river, tap water, sediment extracts, and wastewater at µg/L levels with recoveries ≥85% and ≥85% signal reproducibility, indicating good stability and practical applicability in diverse environmental matrices. While the sensor is, in principle, compatible with portable potentiostats for field use, on-site fouling behavior and true field-ready performance remain untested in this study. A novel electrochemical sensor based on NH2-MIL-88(Fe)/g-C3N4/CNT was drop-cast on GCE (no Nafion binder) and designed for the trace determination of Cd(II), Pb(II), Hg(II) and Cu(II) in drinking-water via SWV with high sensitivity of 0.0789, 0.4122, 0.3251, and 0.2616 mA μM−1 cm−2 and LODs of 4.45, 1.58, 1.93, and 0.76 µg/L in acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 5.4), respectively159. The electrode shows good selectivity against large excesses of common ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Mg2⁺, Ca2⁺, Zn2⁺, Co2⁺, Ni2⁺, Fe3⁺, Ag⁺), short-term stability with RSDs of 1.6–7.0% over 10 consecutive runs, and inter-electrode reproducibility with RSDs of 4.4–11.4%, and it achieves 80–108% recoveries for all four metals in real sewage samples from a municipal wastewater treatment plant but lack fouling resistance data. Compared with the UiO-66-NH₂(Zr)/GO ASV sensor (sub-µg/L LODs in varied environmental waters), the NH₂-MIL-88(Fe) composite SWV platform adds Hg(II), has somewhat higher LODs but higher areal sensitivities for Pb2⁺/Cu2⁺/Hg2⁺, and employs a binder-free, one-pot Fe-MOF/carbon composite that simplifies fabrication at the expense of slightly higher RSDs. The integration of Au NPs within CuBTC MOFs has been reported as an excellent sensing platform, showing great affinity and selectivity toward Pb(II) ions and achieved a LOD of 0.207 µg/L by DPV technique in simulated pure water, which is far below EPA MCL in drinking water regulations160. Through the synergistic combination of porous CuBTC adsorption and Au-catalyzed redox, with linear response from 0.207 to 2.07 × 10⁶ µg/L and selectivity over Cr(III) with a critical gap of no real-sample testing. The integration of a flower-like NiSe/ZIF-67 nanozyme has been highlighted as an efficient dual mode sensing platform, exhibiting strong affinity and selectivity toward Hg(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II), providing broad linear ranges in both colorimetric and electrochemical modes and achieving LODs (3σ method) down to 1.24, 0.97, and 0.58 µg/L in simulated pure water, with wide linear ranges spanning roughly 58–30,800 µg/L, 62–73,700 µg/L, 3.3–86,000 µg/L respectively, thereby demonstrating its suitability for real environmental samples following the regular addition method161. The NiSe/ZIF/GCE exhibits good precision, with intra- and inter-electrode RSDs of a few percent and yields 95–105% recoveries for all three ions in spiked natural waters, in close agreement with ICP-OES results (RSD ≲ 6%), demonstrating practical accuracy in real matrices. Fabrication by hydrothermal synthesis of NiSe, solvothermal ZIF-67 growth, and simple dispersion/drop-casting onto GCE is relatively straightforward and scalable, making the electrochemical mode of this sensor practically attractive even without using its auxiliary colorimetric readout. The synthesis and application of a UiO-66(Zr)-based MOF electrode decorated with Ag2O NPs have been explored for the determination of Cd(II) and Hg(II), achieving LODs (3σ method) of 0.90 and 0.60 µg/L with high sensitivities of 551 and 341.2 μA μM−1 cm−2, respectively, and the sensor has been successfully applied to complex environmental matrices, including soil, wasteland runoff, river basin water, and groundwater samples spiked with Cd(II) and Hg(II)162. The fabrication protocol is practical for routine use, relying on conventional UiO-66 synthesis, in situ generation of Ag₂O within the MOF pores via post-synthetic modification, and simple casting of a Nafion/carbon black–based ink onto commercial ITO slides, which supports low-cost and scalable electrode preparation for heavy-metal monitoring. A recent study has focused on the integration of methylene blue functionalized UiO-66-NH2 with MWCNTs for sensitive and selective determination of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II), demonstrating LODs (S/N = 3) of 1.25, 1.13, and 1.29 µg/L, under simultaneous detection mode, while individual determinations reached down to roughly 0.44, 0.14, and 0.064 µg/L, respectively. The sensor has been effectively applied to the simultaneous detection of these ions in real water and herbal medicine samples, with results in good agreement with those obtained by ICP-MS163. The incorporation of UiO-66-NH₂ provides a high surface area and abundant adsorption sites, whereas MWCNTs and the ratiometric MB signal enhance conductivity, robustness, and signal normalization, enabling practical application to lake water and concentrated herbal extracts with good analytical performance. Additionally, Ta-BTDC-COF/SPE was used with DPV for Pb(II) and As(V) in water, giving linear ranges of 0.5–1000 µg/L for Pb(II) and 25–10,000 µg/L for As(V), with LODs of 0.035 and 0.42 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water, respectively, highlighting the advantages of unique COF morphologies in mass transport. The COF, rich in N and S sites, provides strong coordination and electrostatic binding of the target ions, enabling high sensitivity alongside good selectivity: most metal ions and anions at 100-fold excess cause <10% signal change, with only CO32− noticeably affecting As(V). The sensor shows excellent practicality, retaining stable responses over 6 weeks (RSD ≈ 6%), delivering inter-electrode RSDs of ~4–10%, and achieving 96–106% recoveries for Pb(II) and As(V) in deep well, tap, and industrial wastewater samples, with good agreement to ICP-OES and even detecting Pb at levels below the ICP-OES detection limit164. Recent research has reported the synthesis of a triazine-based COF (solvothermal for 3 days, 120 °C), drop-cast) used as an electrochemical modifier for the sensitive and selective simultaneous detection of Cd(II) and Pb(II) at nanomolar levels via ASV, providing linear ranges of 0.21–22.8 µg/L for Pb(II) and 0.56–33.7 µg/L for Cd(II), with limits of detection (3σ method) of 0.23 and 0.20 µg/L in spiked doubly distilled water, respectively. The sensor was applied effectively to determine Pb(II) and Cd(II) ions in water and food samples, including white rice, drinking water, green tea, marine salt, sugar, dried powdered spices, and black tea, providing results comparable to those obtained by ICP/MS165. The evolution of advanced electrochemical sensors for heavy metal monitoring has been substantially driven by the incorporation of polymeric systems, notably CPs and MIPs. Such materials possess high electrical conductivity alongside flexible structural adaptability and compatibility with selective recognition functionalization. CPs promote fast electron transfer across the electrode interface as effective transducers, whereas MIPs ensure selective binding through engineered recognition sites that mirror the size, morphology, and functional chemistry of the target ions166,167,168. In addition, the combination of facile synthesis and precise modulation of polymer properties via electrochemical or chemical routes positions these materials as ideal candidates for portable and affordable sensor development. polypyrrole (PPy), PANI, and polythiophene (PTh), representative CPs, feature delocalized \({\boldsymbol{\pi }}\)-electron frameworks that facilitate efficient charge transport and yield numerous active sites suitable for interaction with analytes169. Their suitability for voltammetric detection is primarily associated with their reversible redox behavior and the strong chelating interactions that imine, amine, and pyrrolic nitrogen groups exhibit toward heavy metal ions170,171. A novel PANI/yellow 42 dye composite was developed for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg(II) and Pb(II) via DPV, providing a linear response over (Hg(II): 200.6–4212.4 μg/L, Pb(II): 207.2–4351.2 μg/L) with low LODs (S/N = 3, Hg(II): 0.40 µg/L, Pb(II): 1.28 µg/L) in spiked double distilled water172. Clear peaks remained well resolved in the presence of common cations, evidencing high selectivity and strong anti-interference behavior. Real-matrix robustness was demonstrated in lake and groundwater samples using standard addition, giving recoveries of roughly 96.6–103.0% for Pb(II) and 92.3–105.0% for Hg(II) with low RSDs, while repeatability and batch reproducibility were confirmed by RSDs of about 1.2% (same electrode, ten consecutive runs) and 1.8% (five independently prepared electrodes), respectively. The PANI/dye chemistry and simple chemical oxidative polymerization followed by drop-casting onto commercial GCEs make fabrication scalable and low-cost, and the DPV readout on a conventional three-electrode cell is inherently compatible with portable potentiostats for field deployment, although fouling resistance, very long-term stability in complex waters, and any systematic cost analysis of per-sensor materials and processing have not been explicitly addressed in this work. A recent paper utilized the integration of PANI into a sodium alginate matrix then drop-cast on SPCE as a DPV sensor, showing high sensitivity and selectivity toward Pb(II) and Cd(II) with sensitivity of 3.183 μA μM−1 cm−2 for Cd(II) at 10 µL loading roughly an eightfold improvement over 0.394 µA µM⁻1 cm⁻2 at 5 µL, thus highlighting its promise for low-level heavy-metal monitoring in environmental waters173. The biopolymer conducting polymer matrix affords high selectivity (clear separation of Pb(II) and Cd(II) peaks even at 100 µM Cd(II) background), good storage stability (≈97% of the initial current retained after two weeks at ambient conditions), and reproducible fabrication, as evidenced by consistent responses obtained with multiple independently prepared electrodes. The in-situ oxidative polymerization of PANI in an abundant, low cost alginate matrix followed by simple drop-casting onto commercially available SPEs is inherently scalable and inexpensive and is readily compatible with portable potentiostats for field deployment, although the study does not examine fouling resistance in real waters, long-term operation beyond two weeks, real-matrix recoveries, or any systematic cost analysis per sensor, which would be needed to fully substantiate field-ready performance. The synthesis and application of decorated PPy with polymerized phytic acid disposable sensor have been explored for the trace detection of Pb(II) via ASV in real tap water samples in a linear range of 2.07–124 µg/L, achieving LOD of 0.09 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water with good repeatability, stability, and anti-interference behavior174. The sensor film improves hydrophilicity (contact angle ~32° vs 133.5° for bare SPCE), chelates Pb ions through phosphate groups, and yields enhanced stripping peaks compared to PPy and bare SPCE, while selectivity tests show that most common ions at 10-fold excess cause <±10% signal change; significant interference from Hg(II) and Cu(II) can be mitigated using masking agents. The sensor demonstrates good operational robustness, with repeatability over 20 successive ASV runs (RSD < 2%), storage stability over several days (overall stability RSD ≈ 3.1%), and inter-electrode reproducibility assessed on five independently prepared SPCEs, plus real-sample applicability confirmed by tap-water spike tests giving 93.2–98.9% recoveries and close agreement with ICP-MS. However, PPy decorated with NiFe₂O4 shows a comparatively higher LOD (S/N = 3) of 0.81 µg/L for Pb(II) by ASV in tap, lake, and river water using the standard addition method, offering a linear range of 20.7–435 µg/L with a sensitivity of 15.870 µA µM−1 cm−2, which is more than twice as sensitive as bare GCE (4.426 µA µM−1 cm−2) and clearly outperforms NiFe₂O4 alone. Real-matrix robustness is demonstrated by satisfactory recoveries of 98–107% in various aqueous media, and interference tests show that 10–20-fold excess of Cd(II), Fe(III), Zn(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), and Na produces only minor current changes, while simultaneous ASV reveals distinct peaks for Pb(II) vs Cd(II)/Cu(II), confirming high selectivity175. The sensing mechanism combines increased surface area and redox-active Fe/Ni (II/III) cycling in NiFe₂O4 with PPy's N-donor sites to enhance adsorption and charge transfer, but explicit long-term stability, fouling resistance, batch-to-batch reproducibility, fabrication cost, and systematic field deployability analyses are not reported for this system. Between these two ASV platforms, the phytic-acid-functionalized phytic-PPy/SPCE achieves a lower LOD and more rigorously quantified operational robustness, whereas the NiFe₂O4/PPy/GCE affords broader real-matrix coverage, a wider linear range, and mechanistically supported, highly selective Pb(II) recognition, but lacks equally detailed stability and deployability assessment. The integration of PTh with Pt/g-C3N4 nanocomposite drop-cast on GCE has been highlighted as a sensing platform, exhibiting strong affinity and selectivity toward Hg(II) providing a broad linear range of 0.201–100.3 µg/L and achieving LOD (S/N = 3) down to 1.81 × 10⁻3 µg/L and sensitivity of 1.0787 μAnM−1 cm−2, thereby demonstrating its suitability for real wastewater samples.
