The sensor that decorated with Pt nanoparticles furnishes abundant active sites and high conductivity, exhibited negligible cross-response for Hg(II) in the presence of interfering species, remaining unchanged in the presence of K(II) and Mn(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Pd(II), Sn(II), Zn(II), Ag(I), Cu(II), and Fe(III) at concentrations 100-fold higher than Hg176. Real-matrix robustness is evidenced by recovery tests at 0.20, 40.12 and 80.24 µg/L Hg(II) in mixed electrolyte and by wastewater analysis, where results agree well with AAS and repeated measurements (n = 6) at each level display acceptable RSD values, indicating good short-term stability and reusability without pronounced fouling. The synthesis route thermal preparation of low-cost g-C3N4 followed by solution-phase reduction of Pt(II) with L-cysteine and in situ PTh formation relies on standard wet-chemistry and drop-casting steps that are inherently scalable, but the use of Pt inevitably raises per-sensor material cost, and the study does not provide detailed batch-to-batch statistics, long-term stability in real effluents, or explicit field-deployment trials, which remain to be addressed for practical large-scale monitoring. MIPs, widely termed “synthetic antibodies,” have contributed a biomimetic paradigm for selective recognition that has influenced voltammetric heavy-metal detection. In preparing MIPs, functional monomers are polymerized around a template species, followed by template leaching to create defined cavities whose shape, size, and chemical functionality. In the context of detecting heavy metals, these materials are frequently termed ion-imprinted polymers (IIPs), with selectivity governed by the ordered distribution of functional groups within the polymer matrix, promoting high-affinity complexation of target ions and limiting nonspecific binding. Figure 6 schematically outlines the IIP synthesis process, covering template–monomer association and polymerization, subsequent template removal, and the selective rebinding of target metal ions to the imprinted cavities. Figure 7 illustrates an alternative preconcentration strategy based on a Nafion-protected metal (Bi, Sb, or Sn) film electrode, where Pb(II) and Cd(II) diffuse through the cation-exchange membrane and are reduced into alloy films, while organic foulants are blocked at the Nafion interface, enabling stripping voltammetric detection without the need for molecular imprinting. Additionally, IIP/nanomaterial hybrids integrated with metal NPs, graphene, and CNTs provide a practical route to mitigate the intrinsically low electronic conductivity of conventional polymer matrices. A representative approach involves a Pb(II) selective IIP integrated with Fe3O4 and SiO2 for DPV-based trace detection in fruit juice and natural water matrices, where 4-vinylpyridine, 2-(2-aminophenyl)-1H-benzimidazole, and Pb(NO3)2 served as monomer, ligand, and template, respectively, and Pb particles were subsequently leached using HCl. Fe3O4@SiO2@IIP delivered high selectivity in determination of Pb(II) even in the presence diverse competing species up to a 500-fold excess, notably Ca(II), Mg(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Cr(III), Ag(I), Hg(II), Na(I), K(I), Cd(II), and Cu(II), reaching LOD of 0.050 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water and linear range of 0.1-80 µg/L with two well-defined calibration segments that maintain high correlation (R2 ≈ 0.99), thereby demonstrating its suitability for real food and water samples following the regular addition method177. Real-matrix robustness is demonstrated by successful application to tap, rain, and river waters and fruit juice, with recoveries between 95.0 and 105.0% and RSD values typically below 5.5%, confirming accurate Pb(II) quantification in chemically diverse samples under optimized pH, deposition potential, and time conditions obtained via Box–Behnken design. A further study demonstrated selective Hg(II) determination via ASV in aqueous samples using IIP derived from pyrrole as the functional monomer, with ethylene glycol dimethacrylate and sodium persulfate serving as the cross-linker and initiator, respectively. The pristine pyrrole IIP exhibited a detection limit of 20.0 µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water and demonstrated acceptable intra-day and inter-day precision at two concentration levels (%RSD < 10%) in spiked real water samples processed without acid digestion178. A recent paper utilized an IIP targeting As(III) synthesized from 2-methylthiophene and methacrylic acid as functional monomers, with ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linking agent, delivering an LOD of 7.00 µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water and recovery close to 99.7% in spiked commercial, dam, and tap water samples regardless of acid digestion179. In a separate configuration, ion-imprinted chitosan-graphene electrode was utilized to electrochemically detect Cr(VI) in tap water and river water following the regular addition method with a low LOD of 0.03 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water and a linear range of 0.052 – 520 µg/L in the presence of interfering ions such as Co(II), Zn(II), Cu(II), Mn(II), Ni(II), C2O42−, MnO4−, MoO42− and S2O62− ions. Fabrication of the IIP by a single electrodeposition step resulted in strong repeatability and stability, maintaining 85% of its sensitivity after nine rebinding removal cycles and 87% signal retention after 17 days180. A Co(II)-selective electrochemical sensor was fabricated for aqueous samples through electrochemical polymerization of o-aminophenol, yielding a Co(II)-IIP film via DPV. This sensor revealed LOD of 0.094 µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water and linearity range of 0.11–0.88 µg/L in the presence of competing ions including Cr(III), Cd(II), Hg(II), Ni(II), Zn(II), Mn(II), and ions181. Schematic illustration of IIP fabrication and selective recognition, where the template metal ion (e.g., Pb(II)), functional monomers, and crosslinker/initiator form coordination complexes prior to polymerization. A crosslinked network around the template ions is formed after polymerization, followed by leaching with an acid or chelating agent to remove the template and generate complementary binding cavities. The target metal ions selectively re-enter the imprinted cavities, whereas competing ions (e.g., Ca(II), Mg(II)) are largely excluded. IIPs achieve 3–30× higher selectivity than non-imprinted polymers. This Figure was created using BioRender. Publication rights were acquired under a paid subscription in 2026. (https://BioRender.com/kqlsteo). During the preconcentration step, Pb(II) and Cd(II) diffuse through the Nafion cation-exchange layer, are reduced at the electrode surface, and form Bi–M (or Sb–M, Sn–M) alloys within the metal film. The Nafion coating effectively blocks organic foulants and surfactants present in the bulk solution, preventing electrode fouling. The subsequent stripping step oxidizes the metals back into solution, producing characteristic voltammetric peaks proportional to the initial analyte concentration. This Figure was created using BioRender. Publication rights were acquired under a paid subscription in 2026. (https://BioRender.com/faknmzg). The trend towards “green” analytical chemistry has stimulated the search for eco-friendly materials in electrochemical sensing, focusing on reducing the ecological footprint of traditional electrode manufacturing. The driving force behind this transition is the demand for affordable, renewable, and non-toxic substitutes for noble metals and synthetic carbon nanomaterials. Sustainable electrode materials, particularly those sourced from biomass and agricultural waste, feature a unique combination of structural diversity, natural heteroatom doping, and large surface area, which are essential for selectively and accurately monitoring heavy metal ions. The process of converting lignocellulosic waste into functional carbon frameworks via hydrothermal carbonization, pyrolysis, or chemical activation techniques preserves the intrinsic porous structures and the inclusion of naturally occurring heteroatoms (such as O, N, S, and P). These attributes facilitate the pre-concentration of heavy metal ions via a combination of physical adsorption, surface complexation, and ion exchange processes. In addition, the graphitization, along with oxygen-containing functional groups present in biomass-derived carbon materials, further enhances electron transfer rates and provides a reliable interface for stripping analysis182. The conceptualization of biomass-derived electrodes has transitioned from simple waste valorization to the strategic design of hierarchical pore structures and surface chemistries targeting specific analytes. By leveraging the synergistic advantages of biomass-derived carbons alongside other green modifiers, including non-toxic metal films and natural polymers, researchers are innovating high-performance sensors that compete with traditional platforms in terms of sensitivity and selectivity, supporting both environmental sustainability and circular economy practices183,184. A novel technique has been described for the simultaneous synthesis of nitrogen-doped biochar loaded with RuO2 sourced from cotton husks that is drop-cast on GCE. The resulting nanocomposite was used to achieve Pb(II) and Cu(II) voltammetric detection, with a linear dynamic range established as (Pb(II): 1.30 × 103–4.14 × 10⁴ µg/L, Cu(II): 3.97 × 102–1.27 × 10⁴ µg/L), with an LOD of 10.6 and 2.48 µg/L in spiked pure water, respectively185. High surface area biochar matrix combined with uniformly distributed RuO₂ nanoclusters, which enhances adsorption and charge transfer and yields well-defined DPV peaks for Pb(II) and Cu(II) that remain stable in the presence of common cations, evidencing good selectivity and anti-interference behavior. Real-matrix robustness and short-term stability are confirmed in Violatianshanica Maxim extracts, where standard-addition experiments give average recoveries of about 100.5% for Cu2⁺ (RSD 1.63%) and 101.3% for Pb2⁺ (RSD 0.55%), indicating accurate quantification without significant matrix effects. The use of low-cost agricultural waste as a precursor, a simple thermal/chemical activation route, and ethanol/Nafion drop-casting onto commercial GCEs highlights attractive fabrication scalability and low materials cost, yet the study does not report explicit fouling resistance tests, long-term operational stability, batch-to-batch reproducibility, or a systematic cost and field-deployability analysis, which remain open for future development. Another study utilized nano black carbon originating from sweetgum fruit for the detection of Pb(II), Cd(II), Hg(II), and Cu(II) via ASV. The electrode afforded LOD of 1.60, 2.37, 0.34, and 0.25 µg/L for Pb(II), Cd(II), Hg(II) and Cu(II), respectively, while maintaining resistance to interference from Cl–, Al(III), Mn(II), Zn(II), Ca, Co(II), Mg, and SO42–; its applicability was further demonstrated in spiked real water samples186. An electrochemical Pb(II) sensor was fabricated using a carbon QDs/ZIF-8 composite, wherein CQDs with an average size of 2.47 ± 0.52 nm were synthesized from Platanus acerifoli leaves via carbonization coupled with hydrothermal processing187. Under optimized conditions, the CQDs@ZIF-8-modified electrode exhibits a linear range of 0.207–207.2 µg/L for Pb2⁺ with an LOD of 00.008 µg/L in single-ion solutions, while in mixed systems it still affords nanomolar-level Pb(II) detection (LOD 0.004–0.014 µg/L) with well-resolved stripping peaks in the presence of Cd(II) and/or Cu(II), confirming excellent selectivity and simultaneous multi-metal sensing capability. Structural and surface analyses (BET, XPS, FTIR) show that the composite offers a large specific surface area, abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, and a rich pore network, which promote heavy-metal adsorption and fast charge transfer, explaining the enhanced voltammetric response compared with CQDs or ZIF-8 alone. The sensor maintains its Pb(II) response in the presence of common background ions such as Ca(II), Mg(II), and K at fivefold higher concentration (RSD ≤ 2.68%), and demonstrates good reproducibility and short-term stability, with five independently fabricated CQDs@ZIF-8 electrodes giving closely overlapping ASV responses and an overall RSD of 7.79% after repeated measurements. Another Carbon QDs derived from Celosia cristata flower, then coupled to 2-aminothiazole, used as an electrochemical sensor for Hg(II) and Pb(II) via ASV in various spiked samples, such as river, lake, and tap water samples. Nitrogen- and oxygen-rich functional groups improve sensor sensitivity by facilitating electrostatic interaction-mediated capture of heavy metal ions188. A spent coffee modified CPE enabled ASV detection of Pb(II) and Cd(II), and systematic variation of the modifier (spent coffee) content revealed 50% as the optimum, yielding LODs of 1.86 × 10⁴ and 1.00 × 10⁴ µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water, respectively189. In a related sustainable design, an electrochemical voltametric sensor was fabricated from industrial and electronic waste, where arc furnace dust is repurposed into ferrite nanoparticles and spent battery graphite is converted into nitrogen-doped rGO to form an Arc-ferrite/N-rGO nanocomposite. The CPE modified with this nanocomposite exhibits approximately a 3.5-fold enhancement in faradaic current and accelerated charge-transfer kinetics, supporting simultaneous DPV sensing of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) with detection limits near 1 µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water, with excellent linear calibration between 10.0 and 150.0 µg/L and high selectivity against frequently encountered interfering ions under optimized conditions190. The sensor was verified using actual industrial wastewater and benchmarked against ICP/OES, positioning the sensor as an implementation of circular-economy principles, enabling the conversion of hazardous waste into functional analytical platforms for future heavy-metal monitoring. Furthermore, Ca-MOF incorporating terephthalic acid recovered from plastic waste served as an ASV sensor for Pb(II), affording linearity from 0.1-10,000 µg/L and negligible interference from Cd(II) and Cr(III), yet a relatively high LOD (3σ method) of 1300 µg/L191. The analytical performance of methods designed for heavy metal removal is rigorously evaluated through key figures of merit, including LOD, linearity, precision, and recovery. These parameters are essential for validating that a treatment process or monitoring technique is fit for its intended purpose, particularly when dealing with trace concentrations in complex matrices like wastewater. The evaluation of analytical methods for heavy metal determination in wastewater primarily relies on three principal figures of merit: sensitivity, selectivity, and LOD. These indicators are essential for establishing the suitability of a given technique, especially considering stringent water quality regulations that frequently require detection at the \(\mu \text{g}/\text{L}\) concentration range192. Sensitivity, defined as the change in the recorded signal corresponding to a unit change in analyte concentration, shows considerable variation between different detection approaches. As an illustration, electrochemical biosensors may achieve markedly high sensitivity, such as 0.432 mA/mg/L, for a PANI/ZnO/Urease-modified sensor applied to Hg(II) detection193. In contrast, optical techniques typically express sensitivity as a spectral shift. A recently developed microfiber sensor array incorporating an ion-imprinted polymer demonstrated a high sensitivity of 0.01086 nm/nM toward Cr(III), exploiting the evanescent field surrounding the tapered fiber to intensify the analyte-light interaction194. Selectivity, or the ability to detect a target ion in the presence of interfering species, remains one of the greatest challenges in heavy metal analysis due to the complex matrices of real wastewater samples. Researchers have employed various strategies to achieve this. Ion-imprinted polymers offer a powerful solution by creating specific recognition sites for a target ion; a sensor array utilizing this technology was able to distinguish Cr(III) and Ni(II) even when interfering ions were present at a concentration of 1 μM194. LOD is arguably the most important metric for confirming that a method meets regulatory standards, which for many toxic metals are set at or below the μg/L level. The pursuit of lower LODs has led to remarkable innovations. For example, the GDMP-LIBS method achieved LODs of 0.2 μg/L for Cu and 0.6 μg/L for Cr, which are orders of magnitude lower than standard methods and well below the minimum allowable concentrations in water quality standards192. Table 1 reports WHO guideline levels for toxic metals such as Pb(II) (10 µg/L), Cd(II) (3 µg/L), and Hg(II) (6 µg/L). Many of the sensors summarized in Table 2 exhibit LODs below these levels, demonstrating their suitability for regulatory-compliant monitoring. Furthermore, voltammetric methods facilitate intrinsic access to oxidation-state speciation by resolving the distinct redox signals of individual chemical forms under controlled electrochemical conditions. In particular, differential formal potentials, coupled with variations in deposition kinetics at the electrode surface, can be translated into distinct, species-diagnostic responses, thereby allowing concurrent speciation of Cr(III)/Cr(VI), As(III)/As(V), and Hg⁰/Hg(II) within a single analytical protocol. As a representative case, adsorptive stripping voltammetry yields resolved responses for Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in water samples through modulation of the deposition potential or incorporation of oxidation state selective ligands42, and likewise tailoring electrode/electrolyte conditions isolates As(III) from As(V) signals in freshwater samples195. In like manner, mercury speciation can be achieved electrochemically, whereby dissolved Hg(II) is cathodically reduced to Hg⁰ at the electrode and subsequently oxidatively stripped during the voltammetric scan, whereas elemental Hg⁰ (such as gaseous Hg) can be collected on Au-based substrates and then reoxidized to Hg(II) for measurement196,197. The capacity for direct speciation is a defining strength of voltammetric approaches, by comparison, AAS and ICP-MS, which are limited to total metal determinations without valence information and thus require prior fractionation or coupling to chromatographic systems to distinguish different oxidation states30,42. Overall, the intrinsic electrochemical speciation capability (distinguishing Cr(III)/Cr(VI), As(III)/As(V), Hg⁰/Hg(II)) is a major advantage of voltammetry over ICP-MS/AAS, enabling direct multi-species analysis with minimal sample preparation. The validation of voltammetric sensors using real environmental water samples represents a pivotal intermediate stage in advancing these devices from controlled laboratory studies to practical, in situ monitoring tools. Increasingly, researchers seek to evaluate sensor performance in a range of matrices, such as tap water, river water, and industrial effluents, to demonstrate robustness under complex interferences and fluctuating sample conditions. This validation not only verifies key analytical figures of merit, including sensitivity and limits of detection, but also examines operational reliability in the presence of natural organic matter, pH variability, and competing metal ions that typify genuine environmental systems198,199. Extensive studies have employed tap water matrices for validation, primarily addressing issues of drinking water quality and regulatory conformity. One notable case involves a hand-held voltammetric device, the so-called E-Tongue, which is based on SPCEs modified with gold nano stars and was deployed for community-level assessment of Pb(II) contamination in tap water across several towns in Massachusetts. The instrument showed strong practical performance by successfully analyzing 634 household samples, attaining Pb(II) recoveries between 84% and 105% even in copper-rich conditions, with results in good agreement with established AAS and ICP-MS methods198. Similarly, additional studies have verified sensor applicability in tap water, for instance, through an Al₂NiCoO₅ nanoflake-modified electrode capable of detecting several heavy metals concurrently and a conductive polymer sensor modified with bismuth nanoparticles for trace metal assessment; in both cases, the reported recovery percentages were satisfactory, confirming the accuracy of these methods in this widely encountered matrix200,201. The analysis of river water is particularly challenging, as this matrix contains suspended particles, natural organic constituents, and a wide variety of potential interfering species. To address these complexities, voltammetric sensors have undergone comprehensive testing in such environments to demonstrate their feasibility for field use. One notable example is a sophisticated electrochemical platform integrating an oxygen-eliminating electrocatalyst that was validated with samples from Tunisia's Mejerda River for the detection of Hg(II), Pb(II), and As(III), enabling precise on-site analysis without the necessity of purging the samples202. Industrial wastewater represents the most demanding test for any analytical sensor due to its highly complex and often polluted nature, containing high levels of organic and inorganic interferences. Successful validation in this matrix is a testament to a sensor's selectivity and resilience. For example, a sensor based on AuNPs/CPE was applied for the detection of Congo red dye in wastewater samples, with calculated recoveries falling within an acceptable range of 88% to 106%203. The conclusive proof of a voltammetric sensor's analytical competence is demonstrated through its real-sample applications204. This ensures the demonstration of accurate and stable operation within heterogeneous aqueous matrices such as household water, rivers, and industrial effluents, contexts markedly more complex than laboratory solutions205. In such validations, the standard addition method remains the benchmark for addressing unpredictable matrix interferences204. Numerous recent studies corroborate the efficiency of portable voltammetric technologies for rapid, field-level monitoring, yielding data consistent with ICP-MS reference outcomes206. Conducting measurements directly on-site affords key benefits, including minimized contamination and maintaining the native chemical state of analytes207. Recent progress in nanomaterial-based interfacial engineering, in parallel with the refinement and diversification of voltametric protocols, has markedly improved the sensitivity and selectivity of heavy-metal monitoring. Despite these advances, the upscaling of these electrochemical sensors from proof-of-concept in reliable, field-operable analytical instruments, remains obstructed by a series of systemic bottlenecks. Beyond technical considerations, these issues are rooted in the fundamental separation between idealized experimental conditions, and the chemically heterogeneous nature of real-world samples, which weakens analytical precision and long-term robustness of the sensing platforms208. The lack of harmonized procedures for electrode modification and systematic performance assessment across research groups represents a key constraint, making cross-study benchmarking difficult and obstructing scalable deployment of these systems. Although numerous studies report very low LODs and broad linear ranges, the batch-to-batch reproducibility of modified electrodes, especially for manually drop-cast or complex functionalization schemes, remains problematic for mass production209. In addition, long-term sensor performance under dynamic conditions is frequently not rigorously evaluated, with many reported platforms exhibiting rapid deterioration of the analytical signal caused by mechanical degradation of the modifier film or continuous leaching of active constituents during repeated stripping cycles46. The intellectual emphasis in the field is moving from a “performance-centric” approach to a “reliability-centric” evaluation, highlighting the need for in-situ and operando probes of the evolving electrode/electrolyte interfacial processes. Such reorientation is indispensable for probing the pathways of surface fouling and the interfacial dynamics that shape electron-transfer kinetics in challenging analytical matrices. Moreover, the ecological footprint and toxicological implications associated with large-scale production and disposal of nanomaterial-functionalized electrodes highlight the use of circular material frameworks and green chemistry-based synthesis to secure sustainable operation of the technology210. Mitigating these multi-faceted challenges is a prerequisite for advancing autonomously, real-time monitoring platforms that function reliably in heterogeneous environmental and industrial scenarios. Operational stability of the modified electrode interfaces remains a central obstacle for implementing continuous and autonomous monitoring. The time-dependent degradation of sensing performance is associated with both the inherent instability of the modifier layer and the progressive contamination of the electrode surface by matrix constituents. A systematic understanding of these intertwined degradation pathways is essential for translating laboratory-demonstrated sensitivity into robust field-deployable platforms. Regarding the physical integrity of the modifier itself, mechanical stability under hydrodynamic regimes presents a primary challenge, notably during the high-speed agitation commonly employed in stripping voltammetric preconcentration. Furthermore, repeated oxidation–reduction cycles can promote leaching of the active layer, a phenomenon most evident in bismuth and other mercury-free film electrodes, manifesting as a gradual reduction in sensitivity and a systematic shift of peak potentials46. The performance of modified electrodes is also markedly sensitive to environmental fluctuations such as pH and ionic strength, which can compromise both the integrity of the restored sp2 network in carbon-based nanocomposites and the coordination architectures of MOFs, motivating the development of more durable anchoring and coating strategies210. Electrode fouling, the nonspecific adsorption of matrix species, persists as the principal limitation in electrochemical measurements performed on complex environmental and biological systems. The mechanisms by which fouling agents passivate the electrode surface can be systematically categorized. Organic fouling arises from the accumulation of dissolved organic matter, including humic and fulvic acids, proteins, and anthropogenic surfactants211,212. These macromolecules adsorb onto the electrode, physically masking active sites and impeding the diffusion of target metal ions toward the interface. The resulting passivation reduces the faradaic stripping current and simultaneously generates pronounced overpotentials, complicating peak separation in multi-analyte voltammetry. Inorganic fouling involves the precipitation of metal hydroxides or salts, particularly in hardwater samples, forming insulating surface layers that block electron transfer211. Biofouling, the formation of microbial biofilms, represents a severe challenge in long-term in-situ deployments, as the biofilm creates a diffusive barrier with its own local chemical microenvironment, altering both mass transport and the effective redox conditions at the electrode surface213. A systematic study of fouling in seawater demonstrated that surfactants such as sodium dodecyl sulfate, Triton X-100, and dodecylamine cause a concentration-dependent decline in peak height during DPV measurements of Pb(II), Cd(II), and Cu(II), with the magnitude of signal suppression directly correlated with the surfactant's critical micelle concentration212. Similarly, the accumulation of reaction products generated during high-overpotential processes, such as the oxidation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide, represents an additional pathway for electrode passivation214. The development of effective antifoul interfaces has consequently become a research priority, with strategies broadly classified by their mechanism of protection. Physical barrier approaches utilize size and charge-selective coatings that block macromolecular foulants while permitting the permeation of small metal ions. Silica isoporous membranes, formed by the self-assembly of surfactant-templated mesoporous silica, have been integrated with SPEs to exclude proteins and humic substances based on size exclusion, while maintaining sensitivity for Pb(II) and Cd(II) detection215. Similarly, hydrogel coatings based on cross-linked hydrophilic polymers create a hydrated interfacial layer that resists protein adsorption through entropic and osmotic effects216. Chemical antifouling strategies involve the design of surfaces that actively repel nonspecific adsorption. Zwitterionic polymer films, containing equimolar positive and negative charges, form a tightly bound hydration layer that renders the surface thermodynamically unfavorable for protein and organic matter adhesion. These zwitterionic interfaces have shown promise for maintaining voltammetric performance in biological fluids and wastewater matrices217. A third category, sacrificial or self-cleaning architecture, employs materials that degrade fouling agents or regenerate the active surface under electrochemical control, though this approach adds complexity to the measurement protocol218. A notable illustration of the efficacy of antifouling design is the comparative evaluation of Bi₂WO₆ coupled with rGO vs a g-C3N4 composite. The former experienced a severe reduction in average current density in both human serum and human plasma, highlighting the impact of biofouling on unprotected electrode surfaces. By contrast, the incorporation of g-C3N4 composite yielded a sensor with robust antifouling performance, enabling the determination of Zn, Hg, Pb, and Cd in wastewater at LODs of 0.045, 0.030, 0.019, and 0.09 μg/L, respectively, calculated using the 3σ method in simulated ultrapure water and cross-validated by ICP-MS46. Despite these advances, the long-term operational performance of antifouling coatings under harsh and dynamically fluctuating environment including extremes of temperature, ionic strength, and biological activity, remains insufficiently substantiated, and systematic comparative studies across multiple real-world matrices are notably absent from the current literature. Compounding the challenge of fouling, the complexity of calibration procedures and the intrinsic drift of the voltammetric signal further limit the practical applicability of voltammetric sensing platforms. The sensor output exhibits pronounced sensitivity to environmental variables such as temperature, pH, and ionic strength, which can cause the response to diverge considerably from the original calibration model210. For complex real-world samples, matrix-induced biases typically necessitate the use of standard addition methods, a workflow that is time-demanding and challenging to integrate into automated field measurements212. Consequently, chemometric approaches combined with ML algorithms are being increasingly adopted to manage baseline drift, deconvolute complex multi-analyte signatures, and strengthen predictive modeling in dynamically fluctuating matrices219. While the limitations of the standard addition method are acknowledged, the field has witnessed rapid advancements in data-driven calibration that extend well beyond traditional chemometric approaches. Recent years have seen a paradigm shift toward ML-assisted calibration strategies that address the fundamental constraints of conventional methods, particularly their inability to correct for non-matrix interferences such as intermetallic compound formation, which standard addition cannot resolve53. Advanced machine-learning frameworks, including support vector regression, random forest, XGBoost, and artificial neural networks, are now being integrated with ASV to surmount its limitations. For example, coupling SVR with square-wave ASV enabled precise concurrent detection of Cd (II), Pb (II), Cu (II), and Hg (II); SVR attained area-under-ROC values exceeding 0.95, and XGBoost concentration-prediction models achieved R2 values above 0.95 in complex multi-ion samples220. Similarly, radial basis function artificial neural networks (RBF-ANN) have been successfully applied to resolve strongly overlapping voltammetric peaks of lead and tin at bismuth film electrodes, outperforming conventional chemometric methods, including principal component regression (PCR), partial least squares (PLS), and least-squares support vector machines (LS-SVM)36. Beyond peak deconvolution, ML algorithms are increasingly deployed to compensate for signal drift, correct for electrode fouling, and enable adaptive recalibration under field conditions-capabilities that are essential for autonomous, long-term environmental monitoring221. ML and chemometric approaches have been comprehensively reviewed as powerful tools for signal deconvolution, drift compensation, and adaptive calibration, with particular emphasis on their incorporation into continuous monitoring workflows221. These data-driven calibration strategies not only enhance analytical accuracy in complex matrices but also reduce the frequency of resource-intensive recalibration, supporting the feasibility of decentralized, real-time heavy metal surveillance53. Nevertheless, compromises persist: the superior predictive performance of black-box ML models must be weighed against the mechanistic interpretability needed for regulatory approval, and ensuring transferability across varied environmental matrices and sensor platforms remains a significant unresolved issue. The exceptional selectivity and sensitivity of voltammetric sensors are often evaluated in compositionally well-defined buffer solutions. However, extending this performance to practical samples is frequently hindered by the sample matrix. Untreated environmental samples can degrade electrode performance via electrode fouling, and lead to severe signal suppression. In practice, sample pretreatment can be more than a simple preparatory task, functioning as a critical operation that determines the trustworthiness of quantification. Field-deployable implementations can depend critically on their successful integration. Raw environmental waters pose multiple challenges, notably the strong binding of heavy metal ions to humic and fulvic acids, which produce electro-inactive species that are not captured by the sensor response222. Additionally, the sensitivity of voltammetric detection is strongly pH dependent. Since field samples span a wide pH range, achieving optimal detection demands stringent pH control, a requirement that is difficult under on-site conditions where liquid reagents must be manipulated223. Moreover, suspended solids can physically damage the electrode surface, whereas microbial activity can promote biofouling. In response, the field is moving away from elaborate, confined methods toward small-footprint, automated, reagent-free approaches designed for in situ use. A key approach involves traditional solid-phase extraction that selectively captures heavy metals, effectively separating and pre-concentrating the analyte from the sample matrix224,225. Moreover, instead of relying on added buffers, pH can be modulated electrochemically. An anodically polarized acidifier electrode catalyzes water electrolysis, generating protons at the sensor interface and precisely adjusting the local pH223,226. Adsorptive matrix removal with Amberlite is a fast sample clean-up step where the sample is mixed with the resin to scavenge organic surfactants and humic substances before analysis227. One of the ideal solutions is integrated lab-on-a-chip (LOC) platforms, which allow sample pretreatment to be performed on the same platform as detection. A foldable, paper-based microfluidic design brings together sample collection, pretreatment, and standard addition, automating traditional laboratory workflows in a compact format suitable for on-site heavy metal analysis228,229. Every pretreatment approach designed for field use involves an inherent trade-off. Simple filtration and acidification are appealing from a practical standpoint, yet may not achieve complete liberation of metals or adequate suppression of interferences. The next frontier lies in integrating these pretreatment capabilities directly with electrochemical sensor platforms, enabling a shift from basic measurements in relatively clear waters to resilient, automated monitoring in turbid and organic-rich environments. Advancing voltammetric sensors from proof-of-concept laboratory prototypes to regulatory-ready analytical instruments is critically dependent on achieving high inter-electrode reproducibility and durable long-term operational stability. Although nanomaterial-enhanced sensing platforms typically offer remarkable sensitivity, the intrinsic physicochemical complexity of these materials, including batch-to-batch variations in particle size, defect density, and surface chemistry, poses significant challenges to process standardization230. Manual drop-casting often yields coatings with uneven thickness and non-uniform distribution, resulting in significant sensor-to-sensor differences in electroactive surface area (EASA) and electron-transfer kinetics, highlighting the necessity for precise, scalable fabrication technologies such as inkjet printing, screen-printing, or controlled electrodeposition to ensure adequate analytical reliability for large-scale environmental monitoring231. For instance, the reproducibility assessment of ZIF-8/CNT using five independently prepared electrodes for the detection of Pb(II) yielded relative standard deviations of 8.51%232. A major remaining challenge is operational stability, notably when sensors are exposed to hydrodynamic stress and repeated potential scans typical of stripping voltammetry. A 30-day monitoring of the ZIF-8/CNT electrode stability revealed that the oxidation peak currents of Cd(II) and Pb(II) followed a decreasing trend, pointing to a gradual deterioration in stability232. Moreover, the progressive decline in current sensitivity under repeated measurements limits the practical applicability of the voltammetric sensor. As a representative case, detection of Cu(II) at 2.54 × 10−11 µg/L gave five current responses of 0.059, 0.122, 0.059, 0.121, and 0.097 mA, corresponding to an RSD of 34%233. The broad commercialization and on-site implementation of voltammetric sensors for heavy-metal monitoring remain constrained by the intersection of economic feasibility, scalable manufacturing, and the lack of standardized regulatory provisions. Although laboratory-scale prototypes often exhibit outstanding analytical responses, their translation to high-volume manufacturing frequently stalls in the “Valley of Death”, primarily because the use of expensive nanostructured materials MOFs, COFs, and noble metal nanoparticles such as Au, Pt, and Ag, significantly inflates the cost per analysis234. In response to these cost-related barriers, emerging studies prioritize the use of sustainable, low-cost systems such as biomass-derived carbons and SPCEs, which are well established to be low-cost, facile, and highly reproducible in fabrication, making them ideal for large-scale production; mass production has already made SPCEs inexpensive and maintenance free235. Further, flexible and fully printed low-cost graphene sensors fabricated via additive manufacturing are being investigated as sustainable and scalable alternatives for heavy metal detection236; inkjet-printed sensors in particular offer a mass-automated printing process, uniform deposition, minimal material waste, and scalable manufacturing236. One of the most promising techniques for truly industrial-scale fabrication is roll-to-roll gravure printing, which can produce uniform redox kinetics on flexible substrate rolls of up to 150 m length, enabling large-scale, low-cost production of disposable wearable sensors for heavy metal monitoring237. A formidable challenge, especially during the transition from manual laboratory procedures to automated, industrial-scale processes, where uniformity in nanomaterial attributes, film thickness, and EASA across large batches is required to maintain electrode-to-electrode consistency and analytical reliability208. To this end, advanced manufacturing approaches such as roll-to-roll printing, inkjet printing, and controlled electrodeposition are under active exploration to realize large-scale manufacturing of uniform sensing interfaces. Notably, inkjet printing yields thin, laterally uniform coatings and significantly reduced electrode-to-electrode variability compared to conventional drop-casting, which tends to produce edge-enriched films with pronounced interfacial heterogeneity. Electrochemical performance is governed by the spatial accessibility and distribution of the functional layer rather than by the nominal deposited mass, which positions the deposition technique among the primary design parameters in sensor development238. Nevertheless, integrating these electrochemical platforms within established IoT architectures and microfluidic networks for widespread environmental monitoring calls for additional optimization of energy consumption, data-transfer reliability, and long-term robustness239. Building on recent demonstrations of low-cost transducer platforms, disposable electrochemical paper-based devices can be fabricated for less than 0.20 USD per unit using commodity carbons such as graphite powder combined with simple binders on cellulose substrates, without compromising voltammetric performance240. Paper-based electrochemical chips can be prepared for less than USD1.00 per device, with some designs costing as little as USD0.05241,242,243. These screen-printed paper analytical devices avoid expensive noble-metal inks and sophisticated readout hardware, echoing the broader trend toward minimalist, point-of-care architectures highlighted for MIP-based systems. Similarly, integrating MIPs as synthetic recognition elements on reusable carbon or GCEs can be produced at a total cost of less than USD2.10 per test (including all consumables), while the same glassy carbon electrode can be reused for up to 100 measurements, further lowering the per-test cost when the transducer is cycled244. Even sub-cent sensor costs between 0.01 and 0.09 USD have been reported for paper-based MIP platforms that still operate at femtomolar-level limits of detection, underscoring how mass production and simplified imprinting can drive prices down without sacrificing sensitivity245. These ultra-low-cost designs are particularly attractive for heavy metal sensing in resource-limited or field settings, where single-use formats, ease of storage, and minimal sample preparation are essential. Continued development of low-cost conductive inks, recyclable substrates, and scalable MIP integration strategies (e.g., roll-to-roll imprinting or in situ electropolymerization) is therefore pivotal to translating electrochemical HM sensors from laboratory prototypes into commercially and regulatorily viable environmental monitoring tools246. From a regulatory standpoint, the adoption of voltammetric methods is hindered by the gap between advanced sensor technologies and established environmental standards. Current frameworks, such as those mandated by the EU DWD, and US EPA, still exclusively recognize ‘gold standard' laboratory protocols like ICP and AAS247. The recast EU Drinking Water Directive has introduced new lower thresholds for heavy metal contamination in drinking water (e.g., Pb reduced from 10 µg/L to 5 µg/L by 2036, Cr from 50 µg/L to 25 µg/L), creating an urgent impetus for sensitive in‑situ electrochemical sensors that can meet these stricter limits248. The current lack of harmonized protocols governing calibration, validation, and reporting, regulatory acceptance of electrochemical sensing remains a major barrier. Establishing regulatory readiness will require broad cross-validation efforts with benchmark methods in real-sample matrices, coupled with the introduction of enforceable criteria for sensor performance and reliability249. Commercialization demands a staged (technology readiness level) TRL roadmap: bench-top drop-cast electrodes are re-engineered into screen-printed carbon inks on flexible substrates at TRL 4-5, followed by roll-to-roll gravure production at TRL 6-7 to drive unit costs below USD 0.20 and guarantee batch-to-batch electrochemical uniformity. Initial market entry should target early-adopter sectors, such as mining remediation, industrial discharge monitoring, and resource-limited settings where rapid on-site turnaround outweighs laboratory precision. Finally, integrating the printed sensor with a miniaturized potentiostat, low-power microcontroller, and telemetry module will yield autonomous field-deployable nodes, closing the gap between laboratory prototypes and widespread IoT-enabled environmental monitoring networks205,250,251,252. In summary, despite substantial progress, several critical knowledge gaps continue to hinder the translation of advanced voltammetric sensors into robust field technologies. No universal anti-fouling strategy has yet demonstrated stable, weeks-long performance in untreated natural waters with high organic loads, and protocols for calibration, interference testing, and long-term stability assessment remain poorly harmonized across laboratories, limiting comparability and regulatory acceptance. ML models trained on specific sensor batches or local water matrices frequently fail to generalize to new sites or electrode platforms, underscoring the need for large, open-access voltammetric datasets and domain-adaptive algorithms. In parallel, the synthesis of high-performance materials such as MOFs and COFs is often too complex and costly for large-scale manufacturing. Addressing these challenges will require coordinated, interdisciplinary efforts focused on developing scalable, low-toxicity anti-fouling strategies validated in long-term field trials, establishing community-adopted validation protocols, creating rich open datasets coupled with transferable ML models, and simplifying synthetic routes to enable cost-effective production of next-generation sensing materials. The convergence of advanced electrode materials with microfluidic platforms and ML algorithms represents a paradigm shift in environmental monitoring, enabling the transition from discrete laboratory measurements to continuous, autonomous, and in-situ analysis. Microfluidic devices, often termed LOC systems, provide tight control over very small fluid quantities, supporting automated sample handling, reagent mixing, and embedding of sensors in a compact device, which markedly cuts sample and reagent requirements, decreases contamination likelihood, and enhances analytical throughput253. With accelerating industrial development, the detection and removal of heavy metals from wastewater and various water resources have become a pressing necessity, given the substantial risks they pose to human health and aquatic environments. Despite offering excellent sensitivity and precision, traditional laboratory techniques such as ICP-MS and AAS are not ideal for large-scale, on-site monitoring, primarily due to their high expense, operational complexity, and the requirement for sample collection, transportation, and prior pretreatment52,254. Because of these restrictions, there has been growing interest in portable and on-site voltammetric platforms, which combine device miniaturization, low-cost operation, and accelerated analysis with the capacity to conduct real-time or near-real-time surveillance using minimally processed samples210,254. By incorporating techniques like ASV and SWV, these devices deliver the high analytical sensitivity demanded for trace metal quantification, making them ideal candidates for decentralized environmental monitoring applications52,210. In recent years, work in this field has largely targeted the enhancement of sensor capabilities by integrating advanced nanostructured materials and streamlined electrochemical platforms to resolve challenges such as interference from dissolved oxygen and reliance on time-consuming pretreatment procedures. An illustrative case is an “all-in-one” electrochemical sensor based on a dual SPCE system. Here, one electrode is engineered with an electrocatalytic oxygen-filtering layer made of Au nanofoam combined with Pt nanoparticles, which removes dissolved oxygen in the vicinity of the electrode and therefore obviates the sample purging step. The second working electrode, covered with polypyrrole, Au nanofoam, and WS₂ nanosheets, facilitates simultaneous electrochemical detection of Hg(II), Pb(II), and As(III). The system provided LODs that were below the US EPA guidelines for drinking water, indicating its effectiveness for reliable field analysis in challenging matrices, including river water202. In an alternative strategy for streamlining on-site analyses, investigators introduced a local electrochemical acidification technique for the sample solution. This approach employs a platinum grid acidifier electrode that electrolyzes water to generate protons directly inside the measurement cell, thereby eliminating the requirement for external reagents for pH adjustment. Applied to Pb(II) determination using a gold screen-printed electrode, the system provided a detection limit of 6 μg/L calculated based on the 3σ method in simulated ultrapure water, below the WHO guideline value for lead in drinking water, and exhibited a linear response in the range of 10–100 μg/L223. The technological evolution of these analytical platforms has also resulted in fully automated stand-alone systems tailored for extended or remote operation. An illustrative example is a portable flow system based on ASV, incorporating flow cells, a potentiostat operated via remote control, and automated software that supervises the entire measurement sequence, including sensor cleaning and subsequent regeneration. This approach has been applied successfully to the simultaneous analysis of Cd (II), Pb (II), and Cu (II) in river water, with detection limits in the sub-parts per billion (ppb) domains, underscoring its effectiveness for continuous, online monitoring of natural water bodies254. Further expanding the range of detectable metals, a solid bismuth microelectrode was utilized for the on-site determination of Se (IV) ions. By applying differential pulse CSV, this eco-friendly, miniaturized sensor achieved an exceptionally low detection limit 0.055 μg/L calculated based on the 3σ method in simulated ultrapure water with a short accumulation period, while exhibiting high accuracy and strong anti-interference performance in environmental water samples using the standard addition method255. Likewise, a portable sensing platform based on a disposable screen-printed electrode bearing an in-situ deposited bismuth film was constructed for the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II). The approach demonstrated excellent analytical performance, providing linear calibration ranges of 20–80 μg/L for Cd(II) and 10–80 μg/L for Pb(II), with LOD of 1.20 μg/L and 0.96 μg/L, respectively256. Figure 8 reveals a schematic illustration of on-site electrochemical detection of heavy metals in industrial wastewater using portable SPEs and a smartphone-integrated potentiostat. Conceptual illustration of an intelligent sensing framework for heavy metal monitoring in surface waters using SPEs coupled to an IoT cloud platform and ML. Industrial effluents are discharged into the aquatic environment, where a portable SPE device operated by a field analyst acquires voltammetric signals and wirelessly transmits them to the IoT cloud platform. ML algorithms perform calibration, sensor-drift correction, and multi-metal quantification (e.g., Pb, Cd, and Cu), converting raw electrochemical data into concentration- and risk-level outputs based on WHO drinking-water guidelines. The dashboard panels summarize current risk status, visualize historical trends, and support closed-loop feedback actions such as sending alerts, initiating mitigation, and generating reports for decision-makers. This Figure was made in BioRender. Publication rights were acquired under a paid subscription in 2026. (https://biorender.com/jnn7iqg). The integration of microfluidic architectures with LOC platforms and screen-printed sensors marks a significant advancement in strategies for detecting and removing heavy metals from wastewater. Microfluidic platforms overcome the constraints of conventional analytical techniques by miniaturizing and integrating the entire analytical workflow onto a single chip, thereby realizing the concept of a micro total analysis system (μTAS). This downscaling facilitates rapid measurements, minimizes reagent consumption, and supports real-time analyses in on-site settings. Importantly, such devices can be interfaced with highly sensitive detection strategies, among which SPEs have gained prominence as the preferred transducer owing to their low cost, disposability, and suitability for large-scale fabrication257,258. Screen-printed sensors represent a particularly attractive option for electrochemical detection within LOC platforms, providing a means for highly sensitive and selective determination of heavy metals. Their analytical performance can be further improved by surface modification with nanomaterials. As an example, a flexible device based on a SPE modified with MoS₂ NPs exhibited outstanding capabilities for Cu(II) sensing, with a wide linear range of 3.18 × 102–6.35 × 10⁴ µg/L and a low LOD of 1.99 × 10 µg/L259. Similarly, a novel paper-based SPE modified with rGO and a Bi film was developed for the simultaneous detection of lead and cadmium. This sensor exhibited impressive linear ranges of 5–100 µg/L for Pb(II) and 5–80 µg/L for Cd(II), with ultra-low detection limits (S/N = 3) of 0.17 µg/L and 0.32 µg/L, respectively, while requiring only a 200 μL sample volume260. In another application, PbF/SPE for the electrochemical sensing of molybdenum (VI), achieving a remarkably low detection limit of 0.020 µg/L calculated based on the 3σ method in simulated ultrapure water over a linear concentration range of 0.096–4.80 µg/L, which highlights its capability for ultra-trace monitoring in natural waters261. For sample preparation, which plays a pivotal role in achieving accurate detection, microfluidic chips can incorporate functions such as preconcentration. In this context, a LOC device based on a GO-Poly dimethyl siloxane composite was engineered to enable reversible adsorption and preconcentration of lead ions. This configuration improved the sensitivity of subsequent electrochemical measurements using a screen-printed carbon electrode by as much as 30-fold, thereby permitting analysis of samples that had previously fallen below the 0.5 μg/L LOD262. This synergy between the functionalized extraction material and the integrated electrochemical sensor clearly demonstrates the strength of such integrated systems257,263. Integrating ML and data-analytic approaches with voltammetric methods marks a major advancement for the detection and removal of heavy metals from wastewater and potable water treatment facilities. ML frameworks, particularly supervised schemes such as SVMs, artificial neural networks, and convolutional neural networks (CNNs), can be trained on broad datasets to detect weak patterns and correlations that remain undetected by standard analytical tools. The constraints of traditional voltammetric techniques arise mainly from the superposition of voltammetric peaks corresponding to metals having similar oxidation potentials and from the disturbing effects of coexisting inorganic ions or organic materials264. By integrating advanced data analytics, these challenges are addressed via the deconvolution of convoluted signals, the retrieval of key features from whole voltammograms instead of focusing only on peak currents, and the development of predictive modeling frameworks that elevate both sensitivity and selectivity for multi-metal analysis47,219. This synergistic approach not only enhances analytical performance but also facilitates the development of automated, real-time water quality monitoring systems that are crucial for effective heavy metal remediation. The efficacy of ML in voltammetry is fundamentally dictated by the nature of the input data and the architecture of the selected model. Existing studies frequently utilize either extracted features (e.g., peak heights, peak potentials) or complete voltammograms, which typically comprise hundreds of data points representing the entire current-potential curve. While feature extraction reduces computational overhead, training models on full voltammograms allows deep learning architectures to capture subtle, non-linear patterns and matrix-induced baseline variations that traditional peak analysis might overlook265. Supervised learning schemes are predominantly employed to address specific analytical challenges. For example, Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) and RBF-ANNs have been successfully applied for the simultaneous quantification of overlapping signals. A recent study reported ASV method incorporating RBF-ANNs for the simultaneous quantification of lead and tin at a bismuth film electrode, achieving limits of detection (S/N = 3) of 1.13 μg/L for lead and 0.74 μg/L for tin with corresponding linear ranges of 4–150 μg/L and 4–110 μg/L, respectively36. CNNs excel in processing raw spectral data directly. A recent IoT-enabled electrochemical sensor utilized a CNN to analyze DPV signals, enabling the classification of Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II) with an accuracy of 99.99%, and the device achieved a detection limit of (3σ method) of 111 µg/L, 128 µg/L, 87.6 µg/L, and 144 µg/L, respectively47. Furthermore, SVMs have proven effective for complex mixtures; when combined with DPV, SVM algorithms markedly enhanced the coefficient of determination R2, yielding limits of detection of 3040 µg/L for europium, 17,201 µg/L for gadolinium, and 22,652 µg/L for dysprosium266. This demonstrated capacity to resolve convoluted signals from elements with closely related electrochemical behaviors evidences the power of data analytics to broaden the functional scope of voltammetric methods. As water treatment facilities encounter ever more complex contaminant signatures arising from industrial effluents, deploying such advanced analytical techniques becomes crucial for directing appropriate removal strategies and for rigorously confirming treatment efficacy. To progress toward truly field-deployable voltammetric systems, the distinct functional roles of ML must be explicitly delineated and rigorously validated. Peak deconvolution represents a central application, where overlapping signals such as Pb(II)/Sn(II) or Cd(II)/Pb(II) are resolved using advanced architectures like cross-attention feature fusion networks (CAFFN) combined with optimization algorithms, enabling the processing of CV data with coefficients of determination of 0.97 for Cd(II) and 1.00 for Pb(II) while markedly reducing the mean absolute percentage error compared with conventional frameworks219. Baseline correction constitutes a second critical domain, as voltammetric responses frequently exhibit baseline drift due to capacitive currents or matrix effects; here, chemometric strategies such as principal component analysis (PCA) have proven highly effective for baseline correction and noise suppression, exemplified by an arsenic sensor coupling stripping voltammetry with PCA-based analysis to adaptively remove matrix-dependent background contributions and achieve a detection limit of 0.03 μg/L with a sensitivity of 176.34 μA μM−1, surpassing the WHO guideline for arsenic in drinking water267, while more recent automated platforms such as ElectroML leverage PCA–Random Forest (PCA–RF) algorithms to automatically identify and subtract non-Faradaic background currents, which is essential for ultra-trace analysis268. A third functional role is multi-ion quantification, where CNNs and multi-output neural architectures learn direct mappings from full DPV waveforms to concentration vectors, thereby resolving mutual interference among coexisting ions in complex matrices such as industrial wastewater47. Drift compensation forms a fourth pillar, since long-term deployment inevitably induces signal drift through electrode fouling or environmental fluctuations; in this context, recurrent neural networks (RNNs) in combination with online impedance spectroscopy have been explored for drift correction during extended operation269. Finally, field-data interpretation remains a distinct and challenging role, because although on-device IoT integration enables real-time safe/unsafe classification, performance is still highly vulnerable to matrix variability, as demonstrated by a smartphone-based CNN that exhibited excellent metrics under controlled conditions but degraded substantially when environmental conditions changed, underscoring the necessity of robust, context-aware validation strategies270. The shift toward fully sustainable and circular material strategies in the water sector marks a fundamental departure from traditional linear “take-make-dispose” frameworks, moving instead to systems that emphasize resource recovery, waste valorization, and material regeneration. A representative example of this paradigm is the design of an electrochemical sensor incorporating a nanocomposite of green-synthesized nanocellulose and MnO₂ nanoparticles, functionalized with the Schiff base ionophore 5-BHAHS. The sensor enables simultaneous quantification of Hg(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II), achieving detection limits as low as 24.1 µg/L for Hg(II), 13.5 µg/L for Cd(II), and 6.22 µg/L for Pb(II). Its validated performance in real wastewater and tap water, along with pronounced selectivity, emphasizes the suitability of bio-derived materials for constructing sensitive, non-toxic, and mechanically stable analytical systems for water analysis271. At the same time, additional sustainable approaches aim to reduce waste generation and energy demand throughout the sensing operation. A notable example is a novel “all-in-one” electrochemical platform designed to overcome the persistent problem of oxygen interference in ASV, which has conventionally required laborious sample deoxygenation using inert gases. By incorporating some oxygen-removing electrocatalyst directly onto a disposable screen-printed carbon electrode, this configuration eliminates the need for that extra purging step and its associated resource use. The resulting system supports ultrasensitive determination of Hg(II), Pb(II), and As(III) at concentrations below the strict drinking-water limits established by the U.S. EPA, thereby demonstrating its suitability for rapid, on-site analyses without reliance on complex laboratory facilities202. Beyond the mere synthesis of novel materials, a genuinely circular paradigm in sensor design emphasizes the valorization of waste streams as active sensing components. In one pioneering investigation, spent coffee grounds functionalized with FeO were used to modify a glassy carbon electrode for the simultaneous determination of Pb(II) and Cu(II). This strategy not only diverts a ubiquitous organic residue from disposal but also generates a highly efficient sensing interface. The electrode delivered strong analytical figures of merit, exhibiting a linear range for Pb(II) from 1.0 to 800 µg/L with a detection limit of 1.0 µg/L. For Cu(II), the linear range spanned 5.0 to 800 µg/L with a detection limit of 2.4 µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water, confirming that waste-based materials can provide sensitivities comparable to those of conventional sensor platforms272. This concept is further supported by broader reviews, which suggest that steel-based industrial waste represents a sustainable alternative source of iron oxide nanoparticles for electrode modification, contributing to the resolution of concerns about both the reproducibility and the cost of commercially available chemicals273. Voltammetry represents a robust, field-deployable alternative to centralized laboratory techniques for tracing heavy metal quantification, offering a unique combination of high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and rapid response. While the rational design of advanced nanomaterials, including MOFs, CNTs, and bio-derived composites, has significantly enhanced analytical performance, several critical challenges remain to be addressed before widespread commercial deployment. A primary bottleneck is the long-term operational stability and fouling resistance of modified electrodes when exposed to complex environmental matrices containing high concentrations of organic matter and competing ions. Furthermore, the transition from laboratory-scale proof of concept studies to robust, decentralized sensor networks requires a more rigorous focus on reproducibility and the standardization of fabrication protocols. Future research efforts should prioritize the development of sustainable, circular material strategies, such as the valorization of agricultural and industrial waste into high-performance sensing interfaces. The integration of artificial intelligence and ML algorithms offers a promising pathway for the deconvolution of overlapping voltammetric signals, enabling the simultaneous and accurate detection of multiple metal species in real-world samples. Additionally, the synergy between microfluidic “lab-on-a-chip” platforms and IoT technology will be instrumental in realizing autonomous, continuous monitoring systems for wastewater surveillance. Bridging the gap between fundamental material innovation and practical engineering solutions is imperative for the mitigation of global water contamination risks. By addressing these technical and operational hurdles, voltammetric sensors can play a pivotal role in the transition toward more resilient and sustainable water management frameworks, ultimately safeguarding both ecosystem health and public safety on a global scale. This review underscores the necessity of an interdisciplinary approach that combines chemistry, material science, and data analytics to overcome the current limitations of electrochemical sensing technologies. No datasets were generated during the current study. Reda, A., El-Demerdash, A.-G., Sadik, W., El-Rafey, E. & Shoeib, T. Effectively eliminating lead and cadmium from industrial wastewater using a biowaste-based sorbent. Appl. Water Sci. 15, 25 (2025). Electrochemical selective oxygen transfer enables energy-efficient environmental deoxygenation
Source: Nature    Published: 2026-06-23 03:48:16

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript. Advertisement Nature Communications (2026) Cite this article We are providing an unedited version of this manuscript to give early access to its findings. Before final publication, the manuscript will undergo further editing. Please note there may be errors present which affect the content, and all legal disclaimers apply. Deep deoxygenation, defined as oxygen level ≤ 1%, is increasingly pursued to suppress oxidation-driven spoilage and side reactions in applications such as food preservation, yet industrial practice still relies on nitrogen purging enabled by pressure swing adsorption. Because oxygen removal by dilution and displacement depends on maintaining a nitrogen–oxygen concentration gradient, this approach requires substantial nitrogen throughput and high power input. This burden is especially pronounced in cold chain logistics, where refrigeration already dominates operating cost and emissions. Here we report an electrochemical environmental deoxygenation system that selectively transports oxygen from the controlled environment to ambient air through coupled oxygen reduction and oxygen evolution reactions. This pathway reduces entropy generation and operates closer to the thermodynamic minimum than pressure swing adsorption. When integrated with low-oxygen, moderate-temperature preservation, the system reduces total energy use by ~ 36% versus conventional low-temperature operation while maintaining or improving preservation quality, supporting scalable, energy-efficient oxygen management. This work was partially carried out at the USTC Center for Micro and Nanoscale Research and Fabrication. We acknowledge the Experimental Center of Engineering and Material Science in the University of Science and Technology of China. Y.W. discloses support for the research of this work from the National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFB4002001), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22221003), the Joint Funds of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A2081), the Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2108085UD06), the Key Technologies R & D Program of Anhui Province (2023z04020010) and USTC Research Funds of the Double First-Class Initiative (YD2060006005). Y.Z. discloses support for the research of this work from the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WK9990000155). X.T. discloses support for the research of this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52406034). G.L. discloses support for the research of this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52276216). This work is also supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22201271 and 22422506) and the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (2208085UD04). These authors contributed equally: Deming Li, Yi Tan, Xin Tang. This emerging treatment is helping people avoid knee replacement surgery
Source: ScienceDaily    Published: 2026-06-23 03:07:02

A minimally invasive procedure for chronic knee pain is helping some patients find significant relief without undergoing major surgery. For Cynthia Schraf-Fletcher, 74, the results were "remarkably" successful. Nearly a year after receiving genicular artery embolization (GAE) on her right knee, Schraf-Fletcher says the improvement is comparable to the total knee replacement she previously underwent on her left knee. "I couldn't be more pleased," says Schraf-Fletcher, who had the procedure performed by Leigh Casadaban, MD, MS, assistant professor of radiology at the University of Colorado Anschutz School of Medicine. Today, she says everyday activities such as gardening and riding a stationary bicycle are far more enjoyable because of the reduction in pain. How Genicular Artery Embolization Works GAE is an outpatient procedure designed to ease chronic knee pain by reducing blood flow to inflamed areas within the joint. By targeting abnormal blood vessels associated with inflammation, the treatment can help decrease swelling and discomfort. "For treating osteoarthritis in the knees, we often think of medications, physical therapy, maybe a steroid injection, and then on the far end of the spectrum is a total knee replacement. There really hasn't been anything for patients in between," Casadaban, a vascular interventional radiologist, says. "GAE is a promising minimally invasive procedure that may fill that spot for people who have failed conservative treatments but are not yet ready to have a major surgery." According to Casadaban, people with mild to moderate osteoarthritis tend to benefit the most. Patients with more advanced disease can also undergo the procedure, although the effects are generally less durable. "We find about 70% of patients have phenomenal results. They cut their pain scores in half, sometimes more. We have a few patients with no pain at all after the procedure," Casadaban says. "Patients that have tried a lot of other treatments and haven't had pain relief are happy to get back to their normal activities." After experiencing complications from knee replacement surgery, Schraf-Fletcher was eager to explore another option. Looking back, she says choosing GAE was the right decision. What Happens During the Procedure? GAE typically takes between one and two hours and is performed under conscious sedation. During the procedure, an interventional radiology team makes a small incision near the crease of the leg. Using X-ray imaging and contrast dye for guidance, doctors advance a tiny catheter through the femoral artery until it reaches the genicular arteries around the knee. Once in position, the team releases microscopic beads that block blood flow to the abnormal vessels located in the painful areas identified by the patient. Patients are monitored for several hours afterward and are usually able to return home the same day. Doctors generally advise taking it easy for a few days during recovery. Originally developed in Japan a little more than a decade ago, GAE has steadily gained attention worldwide. Since 2021, the FDA has granted "breakthrough device status" to multiple devices related to the procedure in the United States. Research Suggests Long Lasting Pain Relief Early and ongoing research continues to produce encouraging results. "The theory is that GAE reduces inflammation inside the knee joint, and symptom relief can last years," Casadaban says. "Four-year data published in Japan shows that if you have one outpatient procedure, your pain relief can last for those four years. In the U.S., we now have two-year data, which shows that if you have a good response, pain relief can last two years. That really speaks to the theory that we're hopefully modifying something in the joint." Casadaban is currently leading two clinical trials at CU Anschutz. One study is examining changes in knee fluid among patients receiving GAE. The other is evaluating a temporary arterial treatment device called Nexsphere-F, which blocks small blood vessels in the knee that may contribute to inflammation and pain. Expanding Beyond Knee Osteoarthritis Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease that affects millions of people each year and can occur in many different joints throughout the body. Although GAE is currently used only for knee conditions, Casadaban says researchers and physicians are beginning to explore its use for other painful musculoskeletal disorders, including frozen shoulder, tennis elbow, and plantar fasciitis. Story Source: Materials provided by University of Colorado Anschutz School of Medicine. Original written by Kara Mason. Note: Content may be edited for style and length. MiT fusions, TSC1–TSC2 divergence, and stem-like programs reveal distinct origins and vulnerabilities in PEComa
Source: Nature    Published: 2026-06-23 02:59:09

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript. Advertisement Nature Communications (2026) Cite this article We are providing an unedited version of this manuscript to give early access to its findings. Before final publication, the manuscript will undergo further editing. Please note there may be errors present which affect the content, and all legal disclaimers apply. Perivascular epithelioid cell neoplasms (PEComas) are ultra-rare mesenchymal tumors lacking a molecular classification to guide therapy. Here we perform comprehensive multi-omic profiling of an unselected PEComa cohort. We identify frequent MITF rearrangements involving actin gene partners (ACTA2, ACTG1 and ACTB). Anatomical stratification reveals cyclin-dependent kinase module mutations in gynecologic tumors, whereas soft tissue, gastrointestinal, and pelvic tumors lacked mTOR pathway alterations but are enriched for TFE3/MITF rearrangements. Transcriptomic analysis defines four subtypes with distinct lineage programs—melanocytic, mesenchymal, or adipogenic—as well as unique mutational patterns and clinical behaviors. Notably, an aggressive stem-like subtype enriched for TP53/RB1 mutations exhibits high proliferation, activation of embryonic and Hedgehog signaling, immune infiltration, and resistance to mTOR inhibitors, but potential responsiveness to immunotherapy. Single-nucleus RNA sequencing reveals intra-tumoral heterogeneity within this subtype, including divergent inflammatory states. Together, these findings establish a molecular classification framework and identify actionable vulnerabilities in PEComa. We sincerely thank all patients and their families for their participation. We gratefully acknowledge the NETSARC+ network for facilitating the collection of patient samples. We thank the use of BioRender (BioRender.com) for elements incorporated into selected figures. This study was supported by funding from the French Ministry of Health and the French National Cancer Institute (PRTK 2020-046; G.M.), as well as by INTERSARC (G.M.) and MSD Avenir (G.M.).
